proposals.title: Variational Dropout on Hidden States for Bayesian Regularization of Recurrent and State-Space Models
proposals.motivation: In sequential modeling with RNNs and state-space models, overfitting to temporal dynamics remains a challenge. Standard dropout on parameters offers limited regularization for the evolving state representation, which can lead to poor generalization and overconfident predictions. A principled Bayesian approach to regularizing the hidden state itself is needed to improve model robustness and uncertainty quantification in dynamic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying variational dropout directly to the hidden state vector of recurrent or state-space models, treating the state as a latent random variable, will provide more effective regularization than parameter-only dropout, leading to improved generalization performance and better-calibrated predictive uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework extending variational inference and automatic differentiation tools (e.g., DynamicPPL) to impose a dropout mask on the hidden state at each time step, inducing a distribution over state trajectories. This involves defining a variational posterior over states with a dropout-based approximate distribution and optimizing the resulting evidence lower bound (ELBO). We will implement and test this within probabilistic programming systems for structured models, evaluating on tasks like time-series forecasting and treatment effect estimation with continuous treatments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel regularization technique for dynamic models, enhancing their generalization and uncertainty calibration. It will extend the Bayesian deep learning toolkit by offering a tractable method to perform approximate inference over hidden states, with direct applications in causal inference and sequential decision-making where robust state representation is critical.
proposals.novelty: While dropout on weights is standard, applying variational dropout directly to the hidden state as a Bayesian regularization mechanism for the state's evolution is unexplored. This moves beyond parameter uncertainty to explicitly model uncertainty in the latent state process itself within scalable probabilistic frameworks.

proposals.title: Temporally Structured Stochastic Regularization: Modeling Correlated Uncertainty in Sequential Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: In sequential models like RNNs and Transformers, standard stochastic regularization techniques (e.g., dropout) assume independence of masks across time steps. This fails to capture realistic temporal correlations in uncertainty, leading to poorly calibrated predictive distributions and suboptimal performance in tasks like time-series forecasting and causal inference with sequential treatments. A principled model of this correlation is needed for robust, uncertainty-aware sequential learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the temporal correlation structure in stochastic masks (e.g., dropout, latent variables) using tractable probabilistic models will yield more accurate uncertainty estimates and improved predictive performance on sequential data compared to methods assuming independence.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework using Dynamic Probabilistic Programming (leveraging DynamicPPL) to define a latent stochastic process over binary or continuous masks. The correlation structure will be modeled via tractable, learnable probabilistic circuits (inspired by Einsum Networks) or Gaussian processes, enabling efficient Bayesian inference. This structured mask will gate the hidden states of a sequence model, and we will derive automatic variational inference schemes to learn both the model and the mask process parameters jointly.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled method for temporally correlated stochastic regularization in deep sequential models. It will provide a Bayesian tool for learning structured uncertainty, directly applicable to improving causal effect estimation in dynamic treatment regimes and robust sequence prediction. We will release an open-source implementation integrated with the Pyro/NumPyro ecosystem.
proposals.novelty: While correlated dropout has been explored in limited contexts (e.g., spatial dropout in CNNs), a general, learnable, and tractable probabilistic model for temporal correlation in masks for sequential deep learning is novel. The integration of dynamic probabilistic programming with tractable circuits for this specific regularization task is a unique synthesis of Ghahramani group methodologies.

proposals.title: Dynamic Bayesian Matching Networks: Scalable Causal Inference for Sequential Decision-Making in Speech and Video
proposals.motivation: Sequential domains like speech recognition and video analysis involve complex, high-dimensional data where interventions (e.g., feature selection, frame processing) have heterogeneous effects on outcomes. Pre-2026, methods for learning continuous treatment policies with bipartite matching, as in Ghahramani's recent work, have shown promise in static settings but lack a scalable, probabilistic framework for sequential data. Extending these ideas to temporal domains is crucial for applications like adaptive speech processing or real-time video summarization, where decisions must be made dynamically under uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: The core bipartite matching and continuous treatment policy framework, when integrated with tractable probabilistic circuits and dynamic probabilistic programming, can yield scalable, interpretable models for sequential causal inference that outperform generic RNNs or transformers on tasks requiring explicit uncertainty quantification over interventions.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Learning Continuous Treatment Policy and Bipartite Embeddings' model using DynamicPPL for flexible specification of temporal dependencies and use Einsum Networks (EiNets) as the tractable probabilistic inference engine within the sequential structure. The model will learn latent bipartite embeddings for sequential entities (e.g., phoneme segments, video objects) and a stochastic policy that maps these embeddings and historical context to continuous treatment variables (e.g., acoustic feature weights, processing intensity). Inference will be performed via automatic variational methods in DynamicPPL, leveraging the branching and compositionality of EiNets for efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified Bayesian framework for causal sequential decision-making, demonstrating its empirical value on speech (TIMIT) and video (UCF101) benchmarks. It will contribute a publicly released DynamicPPL/EiNet library extension for structured temporal causal models, advancing the integration of causal inference with scalable probabilistic machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work combines causal inference with deep learning or uses probabilistic circuits for static data, the integration of bipartite causal matching, continuous treatment policies, and tractable probabilistic circuits within a dynamic Bayesian model for sequential data is a novel synthesis, moving beyond black-box sequential models to structured, interpretable causal reasoning.

proposals.title: Scalable Bayesian Recurrent Neural Networks via Stochastic Gradient MCMC
proposals.motivation: Bayesian RNNs provide principled uncertainty quantification crucial for sequential decision-making in healthcare and finance, but remain intractable at scale. Current practice relies heavily on variational inference (VI), which can suffer from approximation bias and underestimation of posterior variance. Scalable Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) offers a promising alternative for more accurate posterior inference, but its application to RNNs has been limited by computational and convergence challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modern stochastic gradient MCMC (SG-MCMC) methods, specifically adapted for RNNs, can achieve more accurate posterior inference and better predictive performance than state-of-the-art variational approaches, while remaining computationally feasible for large-scale sequential data.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework integrating SG-MCMC (e.g., Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo or Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics) with modern RNN architectures like LSTMs and GRUs. The method will leverage automatic differentiation (via DynamicPPL) for gradient computation within the unrolled computational graph. To ensure scalability, we will employ techniques like preconditioning and cyclical step-sizes, and benchmark against strong VI baselines on tasks requiring uncertainty-aware sequential prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, scalable SG-MCMC algorithm for Bayesian RNNs, providing a robust alternative to VI with more faithful uncertainty estimates. It will advance the field of Bayesian deep learning by demonstrating the viability of exact(er) inference methods for complex temporal models on real-world datasets.
proposals.novelty: While SG-MCMC is established for feedforward networks, its effective integration with the complex, unbounded temporal dependencies of RNNs is non-trivial and underexplored. Our approach uniquely combines the scalability of SG-MCMC with the structured, dynamic probabilistic modeling expertise of the research group.

proposals.title: Expectation Propagation for Scalable Bayesian Inference in Recurrent Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Bayesian RNNs provide principled uncertainty quantification crucial for sequential decision-making, such as in treatment effect estimation from time-series data. However, exact Bayesian inference is intractable, and current variational methods can underestimate posterior variance, while Monte Carlo approaches are computationally expensive for large models and datasets. A fast, deterministic, and accurate approximate inference method is needed to make Bayesian RNNs practical for real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Expectation Propagation (EP), by iteratively matching local moment expectations, can provide a more accurate approximation to the posterior distribution of RNN parameters than mean-field variational inference, while remaining a scalable and deterministic alternative to sampling-based methods.
proposals.method: We will develop an EP framework for Bayesian RNNs by treating each likelihood factor (per data point or sequence) as part of a tractable exponential family site approximation. Leveraging the automatic differentiation and probabilistic programming infrastructure of DynamicPPL, we will derive efficient message passing updates. The core challenge of propagating moments through the RNN's recurrent structure will be addressed using a combination of probabilistic circuit representations (inspired by Einsum Networks) for certain components and local linearization techniques (like assumed density filtering) for the non-linearities.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, scalable inference engine for Bayesian RNNs within the probabilistic programming ecosystem, directly enhancing tools like DynamicPPL. It will provide a systematic empirical comparison of EP against variational inference and sampling for RNNs on tasks like time-series forecasting and causal effect estimation from observational sequences.
proposals.novelty: While EP is established for models with factorizing likelihoods, its application to the complex, temporally coupled parameter posterior of RNNs is largely unexplored. The novel integration of EP with modern probabilistic programming frameworks and structured approximations for recurrent dynamics constitutes a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: PAC-Bayesian Generalization Bounds for Bayesian Recurrent Neural Networks via Tractable Probabilistic Circuits
proposals.motivation: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are powerful for sequential data but lack formal guarantees on their generalization performance, especially in the Bayesian deep learning paradigm. Pre-2026, while PAC-Bayes theory provides a principled framework for deriving data-dependent generalization bounds for stochastic classifiers, its application to complex, structured models like Bayesian RNNs remains underexplored and computationally intractable for non-Gaussian posteriors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by representing the approximate posterior over RNN weights using a tractable probabilistic circuit (PC) architecture, we can derive and compute non-vacuous PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds efficiently, linking model uncertainty directly to generalization guarantees.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian RNN where the posterior over weights is approximated by an Einsum Network (EiNet), a recently developed PC allowing for tractable marginal inference. Using DynamicPPL for flexible model specification, we will train this model via variational inference. The tractability of the EiNet posterior will allow us to compute the KL divergence term in the PAC-Bayes bound exactly and efficiently. We will then instantiate a PAC-Bayes bound (e.g., McAllester's or Catoni's) tailored to this architecture, providing a data-dependent bound on the expected risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first non-vacuous, computable PAC-Bayes bounds for Bayesian RNNs, formally connecting their generalization performance to architectural choices and posterior uncertainty. It will also demonstrate a novel synergy between tractable probabilistic models and theoretical learning theory, advancing the goal of reliable and certifiable probabilistic deep learning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of tractable probabilistic circuits (specifically EiNets) as the variational posterior to enable the exact computation of PAC-Bayes KL terms for structured Bayesian neural networks, a significant advance over prior work relying on restrictive independent Gaussian assumptions.

proposals.title: Ensemble Kalman Filtering for Scalable Bayesian Learning in Recurrent Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Sequential learning with RNNs, especially in Bayesian settings, is computationally challenging due to the need to maintain and update distributions over high-dimensional parameters. Pre-2026, methods like variational inference or particle filtering can be slow or suffer from approximation quality issues. Ensemble Kalman filters (EnKF), proven in data assimilation for high-dimensional state estimation, offer a promising but underexplored alternative for efficient, gradient-free, sequential parameter learning in dynamical models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the Ensemble Kalman Filter as a sequential Monte Carlo method for RNN parameter estimation will yield a scalable, parallelizable algorithm that provides competitive predictive performance and uncertainty quantification compared to standard variational and sampling-based Bayesian RNN training methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where an ensemble of RNNs (each with distinct parameters) represents a sample from the posterior. The EnKF update will be applied sequentially: after observing a new data mini-batch, the ensemble's 'state' (the flattened parameters) is updated by assimilating the prediction error, using a low-rank covariance approximation from the ensemble. We will integrate this with probabilistic programming (e.g., DynamicPPL) for flexible model specification and explore branching techniques to maintain ensemble diversity, preventing collapse. The core innovation is treating neural network parameters as the state vector in a data assimilation problem.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, efficient algorithm for sequential Bayesian learning in RNNs, bridging data assimilation and machine learning. It will provide a practical tool for applications requiring online learning with uncertainty, such as treatment policy optimization in dynamic settings. The method will be evaluated on benchmarks for time series prediction and causal inference with continuous treatments.
proposals.novelty: While EnKF has been used for neural network training in static settings, its application for sequential Bayesian learning in RNNs, integrated with modern probabilistic programming and ensemble diversity mechanisms, is novel. This approach bypasses backpropagation-through-time for the update step, offering a fundamentally different and parallelizable training paradigm.

proposals.title: Non-Myopic Batch Selection via Tractable Probabilistic Inference for Active Learning
proposals.motivation: Active learning algorithms based on myopic information gain are widely used but inefficient for batch-mode data collection, as they select points sequentially and ignore interactions within a batch. This myopic assumption is a significant bottleneck in real-world applications like scientific experimentation or large-scale model training, where parallel evaluation is required. A principled, scalable batch selection method would dramatically improve data efficiency and practical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging tractable probabilistic models and approximate joint inference, we can formulate and efficiently optimize a non-myopic, batch-mode information gain criterion that outperforms sequential greedy selection.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework using tractable probabilistic circuits (PCs), specifically Einsum Networks, to enable fast computation of joint entropies over candidate batches. Building on DynamicPPL for flexible model specification, we will derive a novel acquisition function that approximates the mutual information between a batch of points and the model parameters. We will employ a branching, tree-search inference procedure combined with automatic differentiation to optimize this function, selecting batches that maximize expected information gain jointly.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded, scalable algorithm for non-myopic batch active learning, directly applicable to Bayesian models. It will advance the field of optimal experimental design by bridging tractable probabilistic inference with decision-making under uncertainty. The resulting software will integrate with probabilistic programming systems for broad use.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of fast, exact inference via probabilistic circuits with batch optimization, moving beyond greedy approximations. This approach explicitly models the dependencies between selected points within a batch, a problem that is typically intractable for complex models.

proposals.title: Bayesian Optimization of Information Gain: A Recursive Approach for Efficient Active Learning
proposals.motivation: In Bayesian active learning and experimental design, the information gain (IG) acquisition function is a gold standard for selecting maximally informative data points. However, maximizing this function is computationally expensive, requiring nested Monte Carlo estimation of the expected information gain over the posterior, and is often non-convex and high-dimensional. This computational bottleneck limits the practical application of principled Bayesian design in complex models, such as those for causal inference or deep probabilistic models. An efficient, scalable optimizer for IG is therefore a critical unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Bayesian optimization (BO), leveraging a probabilistic surrogate model of the IG surface, can efficiently and robustly maximize information gain, outperforming standard gradient-based and random search methods in both sample efficiency and final solution quality.
proposals.method: We propose a recursive BO framework where the outer loop uses a Gaussian Process (GP) to model the IG acquisition function. The inner loop evaluates the IG at proposed points using a scalable, unbiased estimator, potentially based on the reparameterization trick and minibatch sampling from a variational posterior, accelerated by tools like DynamicPPL. We will investigate novel kernel structures for the outer GP, informed by the geometry of the model's posterior predictive distribution, and integrate trust-region BO methods to handle high-dimensional input spaces. The automatic differentiation and probabilistic inference capabilities of the Pyro/TensorFlow Probability ecosystems will be central to implementation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, general-purpose algorithm for maximizing information-theoretic acquisition functions, significantly lowering the computational barrier to optimal Bayesian experimental design. It will provide theoretical insights into the smoothness and structure of the IG surface. The resulting software, integrated with probabilistic programming frameworks, will enable more widespread use of principled active learning in complex domains like causal treatment policy optimization and large-scale probabilistic circuit learning.
proposals.novelty: While BO is used to optimize black-box functions, its recursive application to optimize an acquisition function that is itself an expectation over a Bayesian posterior is a novel meta-optimization strategy. The key novelty lies in explicitly modeling the structure and uncertainty of the IG landscape to guide its own maximization, a self-referential optimization problem not addressed in prior BO or active learning work.

proposals.title: Active Learning at Scale with Sparse Variational Gaussian Processes and Probabilistic Circuits
proposals.motivation: Active learning for optimal treatment assignment or experimental design often requires evaluating an acquisition function over massive candidate pools, which is computationally prohibitive with exact Gaussian Process (GP) models. Pre-2026, while sparse variational GP (SVGP) approximations enable learning on large datasets, their integration into efficient, closed-form active learning loops for very large *candidate* sets remains a significant challenge. This bottleneck limits the application of principled Bayesian active learning in modern big-data contexts like personalized medicine and large-scale A/B testing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining inducing-point-based SVGP models with tractable probabilistic circuit representations of the acquisition function's posterior distribution, we can construct a scalable active learning framework that delivers near-exact performance with sub-linear computational complexity in the candidate pool size.
proposals.method: We will develop a method that uses a SVGP as the core probabilistic model. Crucially, we will exploit the SVGP's structure to derive the acquisition function (e.g., Expected Improvement, Entropy Search) as a tractable probabilistic circuit, building on recent advances in Einsum Networks. This representation will allow for fast, parallelizable evaluation over millions of candidates via dynamic programming. The framework will be implemented within the DynamicPPL ecosystem for flexible model specification and automatic differentiation, with a branching mechanism to adaptively manage the inducing point set and candidate batch selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fully Bayesian, scalable active learning algorithm capable of handling candidate pools orders of magnitude larger than current state-of-the-art. It will contribute a new, practical integration of sparse GPs and probabilistic circuits for decision-making. The resulting software will extend the Stan-like modeling flexibility of DynamicPPL to large-scale sequential design problems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the reformulation of the SVGP-based acquisition function as a tractable probabilistic circuit, enabling exact, ultra-fast batch evaluation. This moves beyond standard SVGP applications focused only on training scalability to address the critical inference bottleneck in large-scale sequential decision-making itself.

proposals.title: Bayesian Inference for Spatially Varying Attachment Kernels in Non-Stationary Network Models
proposals.motivation: Real-world networks, from social to infrastructure systems, exhibit topological heterogeneity that correlates strongly with geography. Pre-2026 models often treat spatial effects as simple distance decay or assume stationarity, failing to capture how local attachment rules vary regionally. A mechanistic, inferential framework is needed to move from descriptive spatial statistics to causal explanations of network structure.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatially varying attachment kernel governing edge formation can be non-parametrically inferred from observed network data, and its functional form, combined with latent node attributes, provides a causal foundation for explaining regional topological patterns.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative model where edge probability is p(u,v) ∝ f(φ_u, φ_v) * k_s(l_u, l_v), with φ as latent attributes and k_s as a spatial kernel parameterized by location s. Inference will be performed via amortized variational methods within DynamicPPL, treating the kernel as a Gaussian Process over the spatial domain. The tractable probabilistic circuits of Einsum Networks will be leveraged for scalable marginalization over latent node variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new class of non-stationary network models with explicit spatial mechanisms. It will deliver an automatic inference toolbox for disentangling attribute-based from geography-based homophily, enabling hypothesis testing about the drivers of network structure across regions.
proposals.novelty: The integration of spatially varying, non-parametric attachment kernels into a tractable probabilistic generative model for networks, enabling joint Bayesian inference of node fitness and local attachment rules from a single observed network snapshot.

proposals.title: Bayesian Non-Stationary Network Models via Spatially Adaptive Latent Kernels
proposals.motivation: Real-world networks, from social to biological, exhibit significant regional heterogeneity in connectivity patterns, which standard models like the stochastic block model or global preferential attachment fail to capture. This non-stationarity in degree distributions limits our ability to infer accurate latent structure, predict missing links, and understand local generative mechanisms. Adapting solutions from geostatistics, where spatially varying kernels successfully model non-stationary fields, offers a principled path forward for network science.
proposals.hypothesis: That modeling a network's local degree distribution parameters as smooth functions of a node's position in a latent space—using spatially adaptive kernels—will provide a more accurate, flexible, and interpretable probabilistic model for real-world networks with heterogeneous connectivity.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian probabilistic model where each node has a latent embedding. The parameters (e.g., scale, exponent) of a local connection kernel (e.g., a power-law or Poisson rate function) for a node will be defined via a Gaussian process prior over the latent space, creating a non-stationary 'degree field'. Inference will leverage recent advances in scalable variational inference for dynamic probabilistic models (e.g., DynamicPPL) and tractable probabilistic circuits (Einsum Networks) to approximate the posterior over latent positions and kernel hyperparameters, enabling automatic learning of the adaptive structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel class of flexible, interpretable Bayesian models for heterogeneous networks, directly addressing the limitation of global parametric assumptions. It will provide a framework for quantifying uncertainty in local network properties and enable new tasks like predicting how degree distributions evolve across the latent topology of a network.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct translation of spatially adaptive kernel methods from geostatistics to the network domain for modeling non-stationary degree distributions, moving beyond simple node-specific parameters to smooth functions over a latent space. This integrates Gaussian process priors over kernel parameters with latent network models in a novel, probabilistically coherent framework.

proposals.title: Deterministic Invertible Graph Networks: Scalable Bayesian Learning via Latent Parameter Mapping
proposals.motivation: Generative models for graphs, such as stochastic block models or variational graph autoencoders, are inherently stochastic and often suffer from intractable inference, limiting their scalability and interpretability. A deterministic, invertible mapping from a continuous latent space to a discrete adjacency matrix would enable efficient gradient-based optimization and exact latent code recovery, addressing key challenges in network analysis, compression, and causal treatment modeling on graph-structured data.
proposals.hypothesis: A deterministic, fully invertible function can be learned to map low-dimensional continuous latent vectors to binary adjacency matrices, enabling more efficient and scalable probabilistic inference and learning for graph-structured data than traditional stochastic generative processes.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture, the Deterministic Invertible Graph Network (DIGN), building upon principles from normalizing flows and invertible neural networks. The core function will be a bijective map f_θ: Z → A, where Z is a continuous latent space and A is the space of adjacency matrices, parameterized using structured transformations to enforce graph-specific constraints. Learning will be performed by minimizing a reconstruction loss (e.g., binary cross-entropy) between generated and observed graphs using gradient descent, with the invertibility allowing direct latent code computation via f_θ^{-1}. We will integrate this within a probabilistic programming framework like DynamicPPL for flexible Bayesian modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new class of tractable, deterministic generative models for graphs, enabling exact inference of latent variables and efficient gradient-based learning. It will provide a powerful tool for network compression, interpolation between graph structures, and anomaly detection, with direct applications in modeling heterogeneous causal effects and treatment policies on networked units.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally inverts the standard stochastic graph generation paradigm, replacing sampling with a deterministic, invertible mapping. This combines the representational power of deep learning with the tractability of probabilistic circuits, a synergy not explored in pre-2026 graph generative modeling.

proposals.title: Bayesian Spectral Anomaly Detection: Identifying and Modeling Networks that Deviate from Universal Power-Law Spectra
proposals.motivation: A core assumption in network science, supported by random matrix theory and models like the Barabási-Albert network, is that real-world networks exhibit heavy-tailed, often power-law, eigenvalue spectra. This assumption underpins methods for community detection, robustness analysis, and dynamical system modeling. However, analogous to geophysical anomalies that violate the Gutenberg-Richter law, many engineered, infrastructural, and tightly regulated biological networks likely operate under strong physical or functional constraints that could systematically break this universality, a possibility underexplored in pre-2026 literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant class of real-world networks—characterized by hard constraints like physical embedding, explicit optimization for efficiency, or homeostatic control—exhibit eigenvalue spectra that are not power-law distributed and that these spectral 'anomalies' can be explained by alternative, mechanistically interpretable generative models.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic framework for systematic anomaly detection. First, we will use a Bayesian model comparison approach, treating power-law and alternative spectral models (e.g., exponential, bounded, or multi-modal distributions) as competing hypotheses, leveraging tools like DynamicPPL for flexible model specification and inference. Second, we will employ tractable probabilistic circuits (Einsum Networks) to learn a joint distribution over network adjacency matrices and their spectral properties, enabling scalable, data-driven identification of anomalous networks from large corpora. Finally, for detected anomalies, we will learn interpretable generative models using amortized variational inference, linking spectral features back to hypothesized structural constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a curated benchmark of 'spectral anomaly' networks, challenging a key universality assumption; 2) a novel Bayesian methodology for network spectral model selection and anomaly detection; and 3) new, interpretable generative models for constrained networks, refining the theoretical link between network function, constraint, and spectral signature.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally adapting the concept of geophysical anomaly detection to network spectral theory and providing a rigorous, Bayesian, and data-driven framework to challenge the universality of power-law spectra, moving beyond descriptive statistics to mechanistic, model-based explanation.

proposals.title: Active Spectral Discovery: A Bayesian Active Learning Framework for Large-Scale Empirical Graph Spectral Theory
proposals.motivation: Empirical spectral graph theory relies on analyzing eigenvalue distributions of real-world networks to uncover universal patterns, such as heavy-tailed spectra. However, current evidence is fragmented, based on small, manually curated datasets, creating a risk of confirmation bias and incomplete understanding. A systematic, large-scale investigation is needed to rigorously test the universality of such spectral laws and discover potential counterexamples or new phenomena across diverse network domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A massively parallel, Bayesian active learning framework can efficiently guide the selection of networks for spectral analysis, enabling the systematic discovery of novel spectral properties and challenging presumed universality laws at a scale previously infeasible.
proposals.method: We will develop a batch active learning system where a probabilistic surrogate model, implemented as a tractable Einsum Network over a latent space of graph embeddings, predicts spectral properties (e.g., eigenvalue tail index). An acquisition function, balancing exploration and exploitation via Bayesian optimization, will select large, diverse batches of candidate networks from a massive, unlabeled corpus for parallel spectral computation. DynamicPPL will enable efficient inference for the evolving probabilistic model as new spectral labels are acquired, automating the experimental loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will transform empirical spectral graph theory into a data-driven, large-scale science. It will provide the first systematic map of spectral properties across network domains and a publicly released dataset of computed spectra. The framework itself contributes a novel methodology for active experimentation in computational science.
proposals.novelty: It reframes active learning from a tool for labeling pre-defined data to a driver of automated scientific discovery in graph theory. The fusion of massively parallel spectral computation with Bayesian active learning for structural property exploration is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Active Bayesian Learning for Dynamic Kronecker Graph Models with Probabilistic Query Policies
proposals.motivation: Modeling the evolution of real-world networks, such as social or biological interactions, is crucial for forecasting and intervention. Fitting temporal models like KRONFIT often requires costly, frequent network snapshots, which are impractical to collect for many domains. An active, cost-aware strategy for data collection would enable accurate longitudinal modeling where observations are scarce or expensive to obtain.
proposals.hypothesis: An active learning policy, which selects future observation times by maximizing the expected information gain on the temporal parameters of a dynamic Kronecker graph model, will achieve forecasting accuracy comparable to models fitted on dense data, but with significantly fewer observed snapshots.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian treatment of the dynamic KRONFIT model, treating its temporal parameters (e.g., densification power law exponent) as random variables with posteriors updated via probabilistic inference. The active query policy will be formulated as a Bayesian optimal experimental design problem, using the entropy reduction in the parameter posterior or the variance of a predicted network statistic as the acquisition function. Efficient gradient-based optimization of the query time will be enabled by automatic differentiation through the model's inference procedure, building on frameworks like DynamicPPL.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a principled, sample-efficient framework for learning evolving network structure. It will contribute a novel active learning algorithm for temporal graph models and provide new insights into which temporal observations are most informative for forecasting network dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While active learning exists for static graphs and Kronecker models are established, the fusion of Bayesian optimal design with *temporal* Kronecker fitting to actively select observation *times* is a novel direction for scalable, data-efficient learning of network evolution.

proposals.title: Convergence Guarantees for KRONFIT via the Minorize-Maximize Framework
proposals.motivation: The KRONFIT algorithm provides a computationally efficient method for estimating large structured matrices via Kronecker product decompositions, with applications in network analysis and covariance modeling. Despite its empirical success and widespread use, it lacks formal theoretical guarantees on its convergence behavior. Establishing such guarantees is crucial for ensuring the statistical reliability of models built with this scalable method, particularly in high-dimensional settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the iterative updates of the KRONFIT algorithm can be formally recast as a Minorize-Maximize (MM) procedure, analogous to the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, and that this framework can be used to prove its monotonic convergence to a stationary point of the likelihood objective under standard regularity conditions.
proposals.method: We will first reformulate the KRONFIT objective and its alternating updates for Kronecker factors within the MM framework, constructing a rigorous minorizing surrogate function for each step. We will then analyze the boundedness of the parameter sequence and the properties of the surrogate to satisfy the conditions of Zangwill's global convergence theorem or a similar monotone algorithm convergence result. The analysis will be extended to probabilistic models where KRONFIT is used for approximate inference, connecting to recent work on tractable probabilistic circuits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal convergence proof for the KRONFIT algorithm, solidifying its theoretical foundation. It will also contribute a new case study on applying MM convergence theory to scalable tensor and matrix factorization methods, bridging gaps between algorithmic heuristics and statistical learning theory.
proposals.novelty: While MM theory is well-established, its direct application to prove convergence for the specific, widely-used KRONFIT heuristic is novel. The connection provides a non-obvious but powerful theoretical lens for a method previously analyzed primarily empirically.

proposals.title: Globally-Optimized EM for Kronecker Graphs via Parallel Tempering and Method of Moments Initialization
proposals.motivation: Kronecker graphs are a powerful, scalable model for network structure, but parameter estimation via Expectation-Maximization (EM) is notoriously sensitive to initialization and prone to local optima, especially with missing or latent data. This unreliability undermines the reproducibility of inferred network models and their downstream applications in social network analysis and recommendation systems. A robust, globally-consistent fitting procedure is a critical unsolved problem in probabilistic graph modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining a consistent, fast method-of-moments estimator for initialization with a parallel tempering scheme that runs multiple tempered EM chains will yield a globally-optimized, reproducible parameter estimate, fundamentally solving the local optima problem of standard EM for Kronecker graphs.
proposals.method: We will first derive and implement a consistent method-of-moments estimator for the Kronecker initiator matrix to provide a stable, near-global optimum starting point. This initialization will seed a parallel tempering MCMC framework, where multiple replicas of an EM-like inference procedure, implemented using dynamic probabilistic programming (inspired by DynamicPPL), run concurrently at different temperatures. High-temperature chains will broadly explore the parameter space, while the lowest-temperature chain performs local refinement, with periodic state swaps between chains to escape local traps.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, open-source inference engine for Kronecker graph models that guarantees reproducible, high-quality parameter estimates regardless of missing data patterns. It will advance the field of probabilistic graph learning by providing a principled, globally-consistent alternative to heuristic multiple random restarts, directly enhancing the reliability of network analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of method-of-moments initialization with parallel tempering for EM is a non-obvious fusion of classical statistics and modern Monte Carlo methods, specifically tailored to the Kronecker graph's structure. This moves beyond standard variational or pure MCMC approaches by creating a hybrid, globally-aware EM procedure.

proposals.title: Expectation-Maximization for Kronecker Graphs: A Phylogenetic Pruning Approach to Parameter Estimation with Missing Data
proposals.motivation: Kronecker graphs are a powerful, tractable model for large-scale network structure, but parameter estimation typically requires a fully observed adjacency matrix. Real-world network data is often incomplete, with missing edges or uncertain connections due to sampling limitations. Existing methods for fitting Kronecker graphs, like KronFit, do not robustly handle such latent structure, leading to biased parameter estimates and poor model performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the recursive, hierarchical structure of Kronecker graphs can be exploited to derive an efficient Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm for parameter estimation with incomplete data, where the E-step's summation over latent edge states is made tractable by adapting the dynamic programming pruning algorithm from phylogenetic EM.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel EM algorithm where the latent variables are the unobserved entries of the adjacency matrix. The key innovation is the E-step: we will formulate the expected complete-data log-likelihood as a sum over all possible completions of the observed graph. By leveraging the recursive Kronecker product structure, we will design a phylogeny-like 'pruning' algorithm (a message-passing scheme on the graph's Kronecker recursion tree) to compute this expectation efficiently without enumerating all exponential completions. The M-step will then update the initiator matrix parameters via gradient ascent on this expectation, similar to standard KronFit but using the probabilistic expectations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled, scalable method for fitting Kronecker graph models to networks with missing or uncertain edges. It will extend the applicability of Kronecker graphs to realistic, noisy observational settings common in social network analysis and systems biology. The algorithmic contribution—bridging phylogenetic inference tools to graph learning—will be a novel addition to the toolbox of probabilistic machine learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-disciplinary analogy, translating the phylogenetic 'pruning' algorithm for summing over ancestral states to the problem of summing over latent edge states in a recursively structured graph model. This connection is non-obvious and provides a new, tractable inference scheme for an otherwise intractable missing-data problem in network analysis.

proposals.title: ActiveEM-Graph: An EM-Driven Framework for Uncertainty-Aware Active Learning on Incomplete Graphs
proposals.motivation: Real-world graph data is often incomplete, with missing node features, edges, or labels, which severely degrades the performance of graph neural networks (GNNs). While active learning can improve data efficiency, existing non-Bayesian deep active learning methods lack principled uncertainty estimation for such incomplete data, and standard imputation decouples data completion from model learning. A unified, efficient approach is needed for joint imputation, learning, and active querying on graphs.
proposals.hypothesis: An Expectation-Maximization (EM) framework that jointly learns a GNN's parameters and infers missing graph data can produce well-calibrated uncertainty proxies, enabling effective active learning query strategies that outperform decoupled impute-then-learn or learn-then-query baselines on incomplete graphs.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel EM loop where the E-step uses a tractable probabilistic circuit (e.g., an Einsum Network) or a variational distribution to impute missing node features and infer latent graph structure, conditioned on the current GNN. The M-step updates the GNN parameters on this imputed graph. Within each cycle, we will calculate and refine non-Bayesian uncertainty proxies (e.g., Monte Carlo dropout, ensemble variance, or predictive entropy) from the GNN's predictions on the imputed data. These proxies will guide a graph-aware acquisition function (e.g., leveraging node embeddings) to select nodes for labeling, with new labels incorporated into subsequent EM iterations.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a scalable, closed-loop system for data-efficient learning on incomplete graphs, unifying imputation, model training, and active querying. It will provide a principled method to calibrate uncertainty estimates from standard neural networks in the presence of missing data, advancing practical deep active learning for real-world graph applications like social networks and knowledge graphs.
proposals.novelty: The integration of an EM loop for data completion directly into the uncertainty estimation and acquisition steps of non-Bayesian deep active learning is novel, creating a synergistic system rather than a pipeline. This moves beyond using pre-imputed data or treating uncertainty estimation and data imputation as separate modules.

proposals.title: Bayesian Compressive Sensing for Sparse Initiator Recovery in Dynamic Networks
proposals.motivation: Recovering the sparse set of initiators or influencers in a network from limited, noisy observational data is a fundamental challenge in causal inference and network analysis, with applications in epidemiology, information diffusion, and treatment effect estimation. Existing methods often rely on complete data or strong parametric assumptions, failing under the high-dimensional, incomplete data regimes typical of real-world networks. This mirrors the core problem in seismic tomography, where sparse subsurface structures must be inferred from limited surface wave data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing network initiator recovery as a compressive sensing problem analogous to seismic tomography inversion, and by employing a Bayesian probabilistic framework with sparsity-inducing priors, we can accurately recover discontinuous initiator patterns from highly incomplete and noisy nodal state observations.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic model where the observed network states are a linear projection of a sparse, high-dimensional initiator matrix, analogous to the seismic forward model. The core will be a Bayesian treatment using automatic variational inference (leveraging DynamicPPL) with a hierarchical prior promoting sparsity, such as a spike-and-slab or a Laplace (L1) prior. The inference will be made scalable for large networks by integrating the model with tractable probabilistic circuit representations (e.g., Einsum Networks) for the prior or approximate posterior, enabling fast, branching computations. The optimization will minimize reconstruction error subject to the sparsity constraint.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled framework for sparse causal structure recovery in networks under data scarcity, directly extending Ghahramani's work on treatment effects and bipartite embeddings. It will provide a new bridge between geophysical inverse theory and network science, yielding a scalable, Bayesian inference tool for high-dimensional, incomplete-data problems with verifiable performance guarantees.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious adaptation lies in formally mapping the network initiator problem onto the seismic tomography inversion framework and solving it with a modern, scalable Bayesian probabilistic programming approach, rather than with traditional, non-probabilistic compressive sensing algorithms.

proposals.title: Active Bayesian Compressive Sensing for Scalable Network Inference via Parallel Probabilistic Queries
proposals.motivation: Inferring the latent drivers or initiator matrix of large-scale networks—such as gene regulatory or social influence networks—from limited observations is a core challenge. Pre-2026, passive compressive sensing struggles with sample efficiency, while standard active learning cannot scale to the massive, parallelized data acquisition regimes of modern distributed systems. A principled fusion is needed to enable adaptive, large-batch measurement campaigns for network science.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Bayesian active learning framework, which selects massively parallel batches of network measurements by maximizing information gain, will enable highly sample-efficient recovery of sparse network initiators via compressive sensing, outperforming non-adaptive or sequential strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic model where the unknown network initiator is a low-rank, sparse matrix with a sparsity-inducing prior. An acquisition function, based on the entropy of the posterior predictive distribution over unobserved network states, will guide batch selection. For scalability, we will employ tractable probabilistic circuits (Einsum Networks) for fast inference and leverage DynamicPPL for flexible model specification. The core loop involves solving a large-scale combinatorial optimization to select a batch of queries, collecting parallel measurements, and updating the posterior via variational inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel algorithmic framework for sample-efficient, large-scale network inference. It will advance active learning theory to the massively parallel, batched setting and demonstrate practical gains in recovering causal network structures from limited data in fields like computational biology and econometrics.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synergy lies in framing network compressive sensing as a batch active learning problem, using Bayesian decision theory to select parallel queries at a scale that makes the recovery of complex network generators computationally feasible for the first time.

proposals.title: Bayesian Compressive Sensing for Scalable Recovery of Network Initiator Matrices
proposals.motivation: Inferring latent network-generating mechanisms from partial observations is a core challenge in relational learning and causal inference. Pre-2026 methods for learning initiator matrices in Kronecker or stochastic block models often require dense observations or scale poorly, limiting their application to real-world, sparsely observed networks where only a tiny fraction of potential edges is visible.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the inherent low-rank and structured sparsity of network initiator matrices enables their accurate and scalable recovery from highly incomplete edge observations using Bayesian compressive sensing techniques, even in sub-linear sampling regimes.
proposals.method: We will formulate initiator recovery as a sparse linear inverse problem, where the observed adjacency vector is a subsampled linear transformation of the vectorized initiator. We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model with automatic relevance determination (ARD) priors to promote structured sparsity and low-rank solutions. Inference will be performed via variational methods, building on the efficiency of DynamicPPL for model specification and leveraging insights from tractable probabilistic circuits (Einsum Networks) for scalable approximate posterior computation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable framework for learning latent network structure from extreme subsampling, directly applicable to causal treatment effect estimation in networked settings. It will advance the field by unifying compressive sensing with Bayesian nonparametric learning for relational data, yielding new algorithms with provable sample complexity bounds.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of Bayesian compressive sensing for initiator matrix recovery is unexplored, moving beyond classical maximum-likelihood approaches. It uniquely combines Ghahramani's expertise in tractable probabilistic inference with signal recovery theory to solve a fundamental bottleneck in network analysis.

proposals.title: Multiscale Graph Pyramids: A Probabilistic Multigrid Framework for Fast Inference on Kronecker Graphs
proposals.motivation: Massive, self-similar graphs modeled by Kronecker products are ubiquitous for representing social, biological, and information networks. Performing inference tasks like PageRank or epidemic simulation on these graphs is computationally prohibitive at scale. Pre-2026 methods lack efficient, structure-exploiting algorithms that can leverage the inherent recursive hierarchy of Kronecker graphs for exponential speedups.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing a probabilistic graph pyramid that mirrors the multiscale decomposition of image pyramids, we can develop a multigrid-inspired inference cycle that solves problems on Kronecker graphs exponentially faster than flat, fine-scale computation, while providing rigorous Bayesian uncertainty estimates on the coarse-scale approximations.
proposals.method: We will define a 'graph pyramid' where each level is a Kronecker product of the initiator matrix with itself fewer times, creating a natural coarse-to-fine hierarchy. We will develop probabilistic restriction and interpolation operators, inspired by probabilistic circuits and automatic variational inference, to map problems and solutions between levels. A V-cycle algorithm will be implemented, performing fast approximate inference on the coarsest graph using scalable Bayesian methods (e.g., via DynamicPPL), then progressively correcting the solution at finer levels. The framework will be integrated with tractable probabilistic models for uncertainty quantification.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, principled framework for scalable computation on hierarchical random graphs, directly translating multiscale vision techniques to graph analytics. It will provide orders-of-magnitude speedup for key inference tasks on massive networks and extend Bayesian nonparametrics and uncertainty quantification to multiscale graph representations.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious adaptation lies in formally defining probabilistic restriction/interpolation for graph-structured data and embedding this within a Bayesian inference cycle, moving beyond deterministic multigrid to a fully probabilistic treatment that leverages the tractability of Kronecker structure and modern variational inference.

proposals.title: Multigrid Bayesian Active Learning for Scalable Graph Neural Network Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Bayesian active learning for large-scale graph neural networks (GNNs) is computationally prohibitive, as evaluating acquisition functions requires repeated, expensive posterior inference over massive graph structures. This bottleneck prevents the application of principled Bayesian data acquisition to critical domains like drug discovery and social network analysis, where data is graph-structured and labeling is costly. A scalable framework is needed to make iterative, uncertainty-aware querying feasible for real-world graph problems.
proposals.hypothesis: A multigrid hierarchy, inspired by numerical analysis and compatible with probabilistic graph models like Kronecker graphs, can provide a low-fidelity but high-speed approximation to Bayesian posterior updates and acquisition functions, enabling efficient and effective active learning for GNNs on the full-scale graph.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where the graph and the GNN are represented at multiple resolutions. We will use tractable probabilistic circuits (e.g., Einsum Networks) to model posteriors over GNN parameters at coarse scales efficiently. DynamicPPL will manage the probabilistic program defining the hierarchical model. The active learning loop will: 1) compute acquisition scores (e.g., BALD) on a coarsened graph/GNN, 2) select and label corresponding nodes/edges on the fine graph, and 3) propagate updates through the hierarchy using multigrid correction cycles, minimizing full-scale inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel, scalable algorithm for Bayesian active learning on graphs, dramatically reducing its computational cost. It will provide a principled fusion of multiscale numerical methods with Bayesian deep learning and probabilistic inference. The resulting open-source framework will enable uncertainty-aware data acquisition for large-scale GNN applications previously considered intractable.
proposals.novelty: While multigrid methods exist for PDEs and some machine learning problems, their integration into a Bayesian active learning loop for graph-structured data and deep probabilistic models is novel. This approach uniquely co-designs the data structure's hierarchy with the learning algorithm's inference procedure.

proposals.title: Multiscale Bayesian Inference on Kronecker Graphs via Probabilistic Multigrid Methods
proposals.motivation: Kronecker graphs are a powerful, tractable model for large-scale network structure, but simulation and inference can be computationally prohibitive for massive graphs. Pre-2026, scalable probabilistic inference on structured graphs remains a core challenge in machine learning and statistics. Accelerating these simulations is critical for applying Bayesian network models to real-world problems like treatment effect estimation on social networks or recommendation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multigrid numerical techniques, when integrated into a probabilistic programming framework, can provide order-of-magnitude speedups for simulating and performing inference on Kronecker graph models by exploiting their inherent multiscale self-similarity.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel probabilistic multigrid algorithm within a system like DynamicPPL. The method constructs a hierarchy of coarsened graph representations derived from the Kronecker product structure. We will use automatic differentiation and branching probabilistic inference to propagate information across scales, solving inference problems (e.g., sampling, marginal estimation) on the coarse graph to initialize and guide solutions on the fine graph. This will be implemented as a library of composable inference primitives for tractable probabilistic circuits like Einsum Networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a significant speedup for simulation and variational inference on a major class of structured probabilistic models. It will extend the toolbox of scalable Bayesian methods, enabling the application of Kronecker graph models to previously intractable large-scale causal and relational learning problems. The resulting software will be integrated into the probabilistic programming ecosystem.
proposals.novelty: While multigrid methods are standard in numerical PDEs, their adaptation as a core primitive for accelerating *probabilistic inference* on graph-based models is novel. This bridges a gap between numerical analysis and Bayesian machine learning in a non-obvious way, specifically leveraging the algebraic properties of Kronecker graphs.

proposals.title: Bayesian Coverage Optimization for Minimal Graph Test Suites
proposals.motivation: Validating graph algorithms is challenging due to the infinite space of possible input graphs and the complex interplay of structural properties that affect performance. Current testing relies on ad-hoc collections of real-world graphs or random generative models, which may miss critical pathological cases, leading to unreliable algorithm deployment. A principled, automated method for generating minimal test suites that guarantee coverage of a diverse property space is a significant unmet need in algorithm verification and machine learning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A minimal set of small graphs, optimized for multi-property coverage, can serve as a high-fidelity proxy for predicting an algorithm's correctness and performance characteristics on large, complex graphs. The performance on these minimal graphs is a reliable indicator of broader algorithmic robustness.
proposals.method: We propose a probabilistic framework where the test suite generation is formulated as Bayesian optimization over a space of tiny graphs (e.g., |V| < 10). A probabilistic circuit, such as an Einsum Network, will model the joint distribution of graph properties (spectral, structural, combinatorial). An acquisition function will guide the automatic construction or selection of graphs to maximize information gain across this property space, balancing exploration with the inclusion of known hard cases. Inference and learning will be handled via dynamic probabilistic programming (inspired by DynamicPPL) to efficiently manage the branching search over graph modifications.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, theoretically grounded tool for algorithm developers to create robust unit tests. It will advance the field of algorithmic validation by linking coverage metrics directly to predictive performance fidelity. The resulting method and open-source test suites will serve as a new benchmark for evaluating graph algorithms.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on graph generation or property testing, this approach actively optimizes for multi-property coverage within a minimal set, using tractable probabilistic models and Bayesian experimental design to create a predictive test suite, not just a diverse collection.

proposals.title: Bayesian Synthesis of Attributed Graph Motifs for Faithful and Scalable Algorithm Benchmarking
proposals.motivation: Benchmarking graph algorithms on real-world attributed networks is computationally expensive and statistically noisy due to their scale and complexity. Conversely, synthetic benchmarks often fail to capture the intricate, non-i.i.d. interdependencies between network topology and node/edge attributes, leading to evaluations that do not generalize. A method to generate maximally reduced, yet statistically faithful, miniature graph motifs would enable exhaustive, unit-test-like validation of algorithms like GNNs and causal inference methods on networks.
proposals.hypothesis: The joint statistical properties of a large attributed network can be compressed into a set of tiny, attributed graph motifs via a probabilistic generative process, and these motifs will provide a high-fidelity, tractable benchmark for predicting algorithm performance on the original network.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical Bayesian model. First, we use Kronecker initiators to capture core structural self-similarity. Second, we extend the generative rules with conditional probability tables, modeled via tractable probabilistic circuits (Einsum Networks), to sample node and edge attributes conditioned on the local graph structure. Inference for model parameters (the 'motif generator') will use DynamicPPL for flexibility, and the synthesis of the final miniature benchmark set will be framed as a probabilistic program that outputs small graphs preserving the learned joint distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated framework for creating compact, high-fidelity attributed graph benchmarks, drastically reducing the computational cost of rigorous algorithm testing. It will contribute a new methodology for network distillation that explicitly models structure-attribute dependencies, with applications in causal effect estimation on networks and robust GNN development.
proposals.novelty: While graph summarization and Kronecker models exist, the fusion of a structural generative prior with a tractable probabilistic model for conditional attributes to produce *minimal* testing motifs is novel. This approach moves beyond simple scaling or sampling to actively learn and compress the joint data-generating process for benchmarking.

proposals.title: Automatic Generation of Representative Miniature Graphs via Probabilistic Kronecker Motif Learning
proposals.motivation: Testing and benchmarking graph algorithms on large real-world networks is computationally expensive and lacks interpretability for unit testing. A principled method to generate tiny, representative graph motifs that preserve key statistical properties of larger networks would enable rapid, reliable algorithm validation. This is crucial for the development of scalable probabilistic inference and learning systems on graph-structured data, a core theme in modern machine learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core statistical properties of a large Kronecker-style graph can be captured and generated by a small, tractable probabilistic circuit that learns to produce miniature graph motifs preserving essential Kronecker product relationships and spectral characteristics.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic model that treats a target large graph's Kronecker initiator matrix as a latent variable. Using techniques from tractable probabilistic circuits (e.g., Einsum Networks) for efficient inference, we will learn a distribution over miniature initiator matrices. The model will employ automatic differentiation and variational inference (building on DynamicPPL) to optimize a loss function that matches key properties (e.g., degree distribution, eigenvalue spectra) between the generated miniature graphs and subsamples or projections of the original large graph.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel tool for the rapid prototyping and unit testing of graph algorithms, significantly reducing development cycles. It will contribute a new methodology at the intersection of probabilistic machine learning and graph theory, demonstrating how tractable models can generate meaningful, compressed representations of complex structures for validation purposes.
proposals.novelty: While graph generation and Kronecker graphs are well-studied, the explicit focus on learning to generate *miniature, algorithmically representative* motifs for testing, using the framework of tractable probabilistic circuits, is a novel and non-obvious synthesis. It moves beyond simple scaling or sampling to a learned, property-preserving compression.

proposals.title: StableSelect: Active Learning with Representational Stability for Robust Generalization
proposals.motivation: Active learning traditionally relies on Bayesian uncertainty or simple heuristics like margin, which can be myopic and lead to brittle model updates. Pre-2026, there is growing recognition that data selection should consider the stability of the learning process itself, as adversarial examples and distribution shift reveal the fragility of learned representations. This work addresses the need for acquisition strategies that promote robust learning trajectories, not just immediate uncertainty reduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that penalizing the selection of data points where the model's internal representation is highly sensitive to small perturbations will lead to more stable and generalizable active learning policies compared to standard uncertainty-based methods.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage acquisition function. First, we generate a candidate pool using a fast, tractable heuristic (e.g., prediction margin from an Einsum Network or a feature clustering score). Second, for each candidate, we compute a Representational Stability Score (RSS) by applying lightweight, automatic perturbations (e.g., via a branching probabilistic data augmentation model) to the input and measuring the induced variation in the penultimate layer's embeddings using a probabilistic distance. The final selection combines the primary score with a penalty for low RSS. Inference and gradient updates for the stability probe will leverage efficient frameworks like DynamicPPL.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, non-Bayesian active learning framework that explicitly optimizes for representational robustness during data acquisition. It will yield a new class of acquisition functions with strong empirical performance on tasks with distribution shift or adversarial challenges, advancing the theory of stable sequential learning.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit use of a learned representation's local stability as a direct signal for data acquisition, moving beyond predictive uncertainty. This integrates ideas from robust optimization and representation learning into the active learning loop in a computationally tractable way, distinct from prior variance-reduction or diversity-based approaches.

proposals.title: Active Learning via Density-Peak Disagreement in Latent Space: A Non-Bayesian, Geometry-Driven Approach
proposals.motivation: Active learning traditionally relies on explicit uncertainty quantification, often via Bayesian models, which can be computationally expensive and intractable for complex deep learning models. There is a need for efficient, scalable acquisition strategies that do not depend on probabilistic inference, especially for large-scale, high-dimensional data. This work addresses this gap by developing a purely geometric, representation-based heuristic inspired by ecological discovery methods.
proposals.hypothesis: That an active learning acquisition function based on the dual geometric criteria of representational dissimilarity to labeled clusters and local density within the unlabeled data manifold will efficiently select informative samples, leading to performance gains comparable to or exceeding uncertainty-based methods without requiring explicit probabilistic modeling.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained or self-supervised feature encoder to project data into a latent space. The acquisition function will compute, for each unlabeled point, a score combining (1) its minimum distance to the centroids of labeled clusters (using a metric like Wasserstein distance for distributions) and (2) its local density estimated via a kernel or the `rho` value from the Density Peaks clustering algorithm (Rodriguez & Laio, 2014). We will integrate this into the DynamicPPL framework for flexible experimentation and use probabilistic circuits (e.g., Einsum Networks) as one benchmark model to compare against Bayesian uncertainty baselines.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel, computationally efficient active learning algorithm that bypasses the need for explicit uncertainty estimation. It will contribute a new perspective on informativeness based on data geometry and representation structure, expanding the toolbox for treatment strategies in sequential learning. The work will also formalize a valuable analogy between ecological discovery and machine learning.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct translation of an ecological heuristic for species discovery—seeking distinct yet abundant specimens—into a non-Bayesian active learning criterion. Unlike prior geometry-based methods, it explicitly combines representational disagreement with density-based representativeness in a single, principled acquisition function.

proposals.title: Topology-Aware Active Learning for Heterogeneous Graphs via Probabilistic Circuit Acquisition Functions
proposals.motivation: Active learning on large graphs is crucial for tasks like node classification where labeling is expensive, but traditional uncertainty-based acquisition functions are computationally intractable for complex Bayesian models on massive networks. Existing graph-based active learning methods often overlook the profound heterogeneity in local topological structure, such as the non-stationary degree distributions between hubs and periphery, which governs information flow and representativeness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acquisition functions based directly on local topological properties (e.g., degree, betweenness, community membership) and their heterogeneity will yield more efficient and scalable label acquisition than model-uncertainty proxies, by strategically querying nodes that are structurally critical or diverse.
proposals.method: We will develop a model-agnostic framework where acquisition scores are computed via a learned probabilistic circuit over node features and topological summaries. Leveraging Einsum Networks for fast inference, we will define a tractable joint distribution P(structure, latent utility). A dynamic probabilistic programming model (using DynamicPPL) will allow the acquisition policy to adapt as the labeled set grows, branching between strategies for hubs versus peripheral nodes. The final query strategy will maximize expected information gain on the graph's inferred bipartite structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, model-agnostic active learning algorithm for massive graphs where Bayesian uncertainty is prohibitive. It will provide a formal framework for treating graph heterogeneity as a first-class citizen in acquisition design, with open-source implementations. The resulting method will be validated on social, citation, and biological interactome networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in replacing model uncertainty with learned, topology-aware utility functions via tractable probabilistic circuits, explicitly modeling non-stationary structural roles to drive acquisition, a departure from prior graph-agnostic or uncertainty-only methods.

proposals.title: Bayesian Adaptive Cluster Sampling for Active Learning with Scarce Unlabeled Data
proposals.motivation: Active learning typically assumes a large, replenishable pool of unlabeled data, but many real-world applications in science and medicine involve small, static, and expensive-to-obtain datasets. In these data-scarce regimes, conventional uncertainty sampling can waste precious queries on isolated outliers, failing to model the underlying structure of informative instances. This work addresses the critical challenge of maximizing knowledge extraction when the query budget is severely limited relative to the pool size.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the spatial clustering of informative instances within a fixed, small data pool using principles from adaptive cluster sampling and sequential Bayesian spatial modeling, we can develop an active learning strategy that significantly outperforms standard uncertainty-based methods in data-scarce scenarios.
proposals.method: We will develop a sequential Bayesian model that treats the feature space as a landscape, using initial queries to infer a probabilistic spatial map of 'data density' for informative instances, akin to species distribution modeling. This model will be implemented within a dynamic probabilistic programming framework (e.g., DynamicPPL) for efficient inference. The query strategy will maximize an acquisition function combining predictive uncertainty (from a tractable probabilistic circuit model like an Einsum Network) and the inferred local density, guiding sampling towards dense clusters of high-uncertainty points. The process will be iterative and budget-aware, with the spatial model updated after each query.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel active learning framework specifically designed for severely data-scarce environments, formalizing the trade-off between exploration and exploitation of the data pool's structure. It will contribute a new class of acquisition functions and a practical methodology for domains where unlabeled data is a finite, non-renewable resource, advancing the field of efficient experimental design.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct adaptation of ecological survey techniques (adaptive cluster sampling) to active learning, moving beyond point-wise uncertainty to model and exploit the spatial correlation of informativeness within a small, static pool—a paradigm shift from the standard 'abundant pool' assumption.

proposals.title: Bayesian Optimal Design for Exhaustible-Resource Few-Shot Learning
proposals.motivation: Few-shot learning typically assumes a pre-constructed support set, ignoring the critical, costly process of initially gathering labeled data. In real-world scenarios like scientific discovery or medical diagnosis, the pool of available unlabeled data for a new task is often extremely small and non-replenishable. Standard active learning, designed for large pools and iterative querying, fails under this exhaustible-resource constraint, creating a need for a holistic cold-start protocol.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian optimal experimental design framework, which jointly models informativeness for immediate discrimination and long-term task representativeness, can construct an initial few-shot support set from a fully consumed, tiny unlabeled pool that outperforms standard acquisition strategies in final task performance.
proposals.method: We will formulate the selection as a Bayesian decision problem under uncertainty, leveraging probabilistic models like tractable Einsum Networks for fast posterior inference over data utility.
