The acquisition function will be a sum of two terms: 1) an information-theoretic term (e.g., BALD) for discriminative power, and 2) a diversity term based on probabilistic embeddings from a pre-trained model, ensuring the selected set spans the constrained data manifold. We will implement this within a dynamic probabilistic programming framework (e.g., DynamicPPL) to enable flexible modeling of the sequential, non-replenishable selection process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, Bayesian method for the fused problem of dataset construction and model initialization in data-scarce environments. It will yield a practical algorithm for cold-start few-shot learning with theoretical guarantees on resource optimality, directly applicable to fields like personalized medicine where initial patient cohorts are small.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond iterative active learning by framing the entire unlabeled pool as a single, exhaustible batch to be optimally partitioned, merging optimal experimental design with few-shot initialization in a way not addressed by prior work.

proposals.title: Active Bayesian Learning with Probabilistic Circuits for Data-Scarce Decision-Making
proposals.motivation: Active learning traditionally assumes a large, static pool of unlabeled data from which to query. However, in high-stakes domains like personalized medicine or experimental design, acquiring new unlabeled data is expensive or ethically constrained, creating a 'data-scarce' active learning regime. This scenario is poorly addressed by existing methods, which are inefficient when the candidate pool is very small and each query must be maximally informative for downstream tasks like treatment effect estimation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian active learning framework, which jointly models epistemic uncertainty and task-specific utility (e.g., causal effect variance reduction) using tractable probabilistic models, can optimally select queries from a minimal unlabeled pool to maximize performance in data-scarce settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework using tractable probabilistic circuits (PCs), specifically Einsum Networks, for efficient inference and uncertainty quantification. The query strategy will be formulated as a Bayesian decision problem within DynamicPPL, balancing information gain (via PC-entropy) and expected improvement in a downstream task model (e.g., a heterogeneous treatment effect model). We will introduce a novel acquisition function that performs probabilistic inference over the branching structure of the PC and the parameters of the task model to select the data point that minimizes the expected posterior variance of the target estimand.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable active learning methodology for scenarios where unlabeled data is severely limited. It will contribute new acquisition functions tailored for decision-focused learning and demonstrate application in causal inference tasks, such as learning optimal treatment policies with minimal patient intervention.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of tractable probabilistic circuits for fast, exact marginal inference within a dynamic probabilistic programming framework, enabling joint optimization for epistemic uncertainty and specific task performance under extreme data scarcity, a combination not explored in pre-2026 active learning literature.

proposals.title: Ensemble Disagreement as Epistemic Uncertainty for Efficient Deep Active Learning
proposals.motivation: Deep active learning is crucial for reducing labeling costs in data-intensive applications, but its reliance on Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) for uncertainty quantification introduces significant computational and implementation complexity. This limits widespread adoption. The success of ensemble methods in providing robust uncertainty estimates in fields like meteorology suggests a simpler, more scalable alternative is possible for machine learning.
proposals.hypothesis: The predictive variance across an ensemble of standard, independently trained neural networks serves as a robust and computationally efficient proxy for epistemic uncertainty, enabling deep active learning performance that matches or exceeds more complex Bayesian approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a rigorous active learning framework using a committee of standard neural networks (e.g., ResNets, Transformers) with varied initializations and data orders to induce functional diversity. At each acquisition step, we will compute the predictive variance over unlabeled data points using the ensemble's outputs. We will then select points with the highest variance for labeling, formalizing this within a probabilistic query-by-committee algorithm. The performance will be benchmarked against state-of-the-art BNNs and deep ensembles on standard vision and NLP datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, high-performance active learning method that bypasses the need for specialized Bayesian architectures, making uncertainty-driven data acquisition more accessible. It will also offer a formal analysis linking ensemble diversity to epistemic uncertainty, bridging ideas from ensemble forecasting and probabilistic machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While deep ensembles are known for predictive performance, their systematic use as a direct, efficient proxy for epistemic uncertainty in active learning—challenging the necessity of BNNs—is a non-obvious synthesis of ideas from ensemble forecasting and probabilistic inference.

proposals.title: Bayesian Active Learning with Peer Prediction: A Probabilistic Circuit Approach to Crowdsourced Label Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Active learning traditionally assumes a single, perfect annotator, which is unrealistic for many real-world applications where data is labeled by a heterogeneous, noisy crowd. Existing methods for learning from multiple annotators often rely on having some ground truth for validation or use simple aggregation, failing to incentivize truthful reporting or model complex annotator biases. This gap limits the scalability and robustness of active learning in crowdsourcing and citizen science platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a peer-prediction mechanism, inspired by the Bayesian Truth Serum (BTS), into a probabilistic active learning framework will yield more robust and efficient label acquisition by truthfully eliciting and probabilistically fusing information from multiple unreliable annotators without requiring ground truth.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic probabilistic model within a framework like DynamicPPL, treating annotator reliability and the latent true label as latent variables. For each query, we will present it to a panel of annotators and use a BTS-inspired scoring rule within the inference procedure. The core learner will be a tractable probabilistic circuit (e.g., an Einsum Network) to model the data distribution and the joint distribution over annotator responses, enabling fast, automatic Bayesian inference over labels and reliability. The active learning criterion will be extended to consider both model uncertainty and the estimated information gain from the annotator panel.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, end-to-end Bayesian active learning system for noisy, crowdsourced settings that does not require gold-standard labels. It will advance the field by providing a principled integration of incentive-aware peer prediction with scalable probabilistic learning, yielding a more practical and theoretically grounded tool for data acquisition.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal analogy drawn between peer-prediction mechanisms from economic theory and Bayesian active learning, creating a unified probabilistic model that jointly performs incentive-compatible elicitation, reliability estimation, and data-efficient learning in a single, tractable inference loop.

proposals.title: Bayesian Pruning of Data and Annotators via Probabilistic Circuits for Robust Dataset Distillation
proposals.motivation: Modern datasets are often constructed via crowdsourcing, leading to noisy and biased labels from multiple annotators. This noise degrades model performance and inflates computational costs. Existing dataset distillation and pruning methods typically assume clean labels, failing to leverage the rich signal in annotator disagreement for robust dataset refinement.
proposals.hypothesis: A joint Bayesian model of annotator reliability and data point informativeness can be used to simultaneously prune redundant training examples and unreliable annotators, distilling a dataset that yields more robust and efficient model convergence.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical probabilistic model where the true label of each data point is a latent variable, and each annotator's responses are generated via a confusion matrix. We will represent this model using tractable probabilistic circuits (e.g., Einsum Networks) for fast inference. A novel, joint pruning criterion will be derived from the posterior, combining predictive entropy (data uncertainty) and annotator entropy (annotator uncertainty), enabling automatic, iterative distillation of the dataset and the annotator pool.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable framework for creating high-confidence, compact training sets from noisy multi-annotator data. It will advance the fields of dataset distillation and learning from crowds by introducing a unified Bayesian treatment of data and annotator quality.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the synergistic, joint pruning of data points and annotators within a single probabilistic framework, inverting the active learning paradigm to use multi-annotator disagreement as a direct signal for distillation, not just for label aggregation.

proposals.title: Bayesian Active Learning for Audio-Video via Geophysical Wavefield Analogy
proposals.motivation: Active learning for high-dimensional, structured data like audio and video remains computationally challenging, as standard uncertainty sampling methods struggle with the complex spatiotemporal dependencies. Meanwhile, geophysics has developed sophisticated Bayesian experimental design methods for efficiently characterizing seismic wavefields, which share structural similarities with spectrograms and video volumes. Adapting these proven techniques could enable more sample-efficient learning for costly audio-video annotation tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling audio spectrograms and video spatiotemporal volumes as analogous to geophysical wavefields, a Bayesian active learning framework using optimal sensor placement strategies can select more informative samples than standard methods, significantly improving data efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a tractable probabilistic model, building on Einsum Networks or DynamicPPL, over spectral/spatiotemporal feature embeddings from a deep encoder. Inspired by geophysical optimal design, we will formulate the acquisition function as a Bayesian optimization problem for 'sensor' (i.e., clip/segment) placement, maximizing mutual information between selected observations and the model parameters. This will leverage automatic inference and branching processes for scalable, dynamic decision-making across the data stream.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled Bayesian active learning framework specifically tailored for audio and video data. It will advance methods for efficient learning from complex, high-dimensional streams and provide a concrete bridge between geophysical signal processing and machine learning.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct conceptual and technical analogy drawn from geophysical wavefield characterization to audio-video active learning, translating optimal sensor placement into a novel acquisition strategy for deep probabilistic models.

proposals.title: Two-Stage Adaptive Computation for Scalable Bayesian Active Learning
proposals.motivation: Bayesian active learning provides a principled framework for data-efficient machine learning but is computationally prohibitive for large datasets and complex models like deep neural networks. The core bottleneck is evaluating an expensive acquisition function, such as BALD, over the entire unlabeled pool for each iteration, which requires costly posterior approximations via MCMC or deep ensembles. This severely limits the practical application of Bayesian active learning in modern settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computationally cheap surrogate Bayesian model can efficiently pre-screen the data pool to identify a high-promise candidate subset, such that evaluating the full, expensive target model only on this subset preserves the quality of data selection while drastically reducing per-iteration cost.
proposals.method: We propose a framework inspired by adaptive ecological survey. Stage 1: A fast, tractable surrogate model (e.g., a last-layer Laplace approximation, a small Probabilistic Circuit like an Einsum Network, or a tiny ensemble) rapidly computes a proxy for epistemic uncertainty across the full unlabeled pool. Stage 2: Only the top-k points from this screening are passed to the full target model (e.g., a Bayesian neural network with DynamicPPL/HMC inference) for precise acquisition function calculation. We will develop automatic rules for setting k and for validating the surrogate's screening fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical algorithm that makes Bayesian active learning scalable to larger datasets and more complex models without sacrificing its decision-theoretic rigor. It will provide a formal analysis of the trade-off between computational savings and selection performance, grounded in an ecological sampling analogy.
proposals.novelty: While multi-fidelity and bandit methods exist, the explicit decoupling of screening and final evaluation using distinct Bayesian models, and the conceptual bridge to adaptive ecological design, is novel. The use of ultra-fast tractable models (e.g., Probabilistic Circuits) as screening surrogates for deep Bayesian models is an unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Tractable Surrogate Processes for Provable Active Learning with Bayesian Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) offer principled uncertainty quantification crucial for active learning, but their predictive distributions are intractable, making acquisition function optimization and theoretical analysis prohibitively expensive. This intractability forces a reliance on crude approximations, undermining the theoretical guarantees and sample efficiency that motivate Bayesian active learning. A method to faithfully yet efficiently emulate a BNN's predictive stochastic process is needed to unlock its potential for data-efficient learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned, context-aware surrogate stochastic process (e.g., a Gaussian Process with a neural kernel) can accurately approximate the predictive distribution of a BNN, enabling the direct application of efficient, theoretically grounded active learning acquisition functions and guarantees that are otherwise intractable.
proposals.method: We will develop a system that jointly trains a BNN and a meta-model (e.g., a hypernetwork or amortized inference network) that maps a dataset context to the parameters of a tractable surrogate process, such as a Gaussian Process or an Einsum Network. This meta-model will be trained to minimize a divergence between the surrogate's predictive distribution and the BNN's posterior predictive, approximated via samples. For active learning, acquisition functions (e.g., BALD, expected improvement) will be computed analytically on the rapidly updatable surrogate. Selected points will then refine the BNN (via standard Bayesian inference) and the meta-model in a loop, leveraging dynamic probabilistic programming frameworks like DynamicPPL for flexibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable framework for active learning with complex neural models that maintains strong theoretical grounding. It will deliver a practical algorithm with provable approximation guarantees, bridging the gap between the flexibility of deep learning and the tractability of classical Bayesian nonparametrics for sequential decision-making.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the meta-learning of a *faithful*, rapidly updatable surrogate process specifically for active learning, moving beyond static or moment-matched approximations. This creates a synergistic loop where the BNN's representation improves the surrogate, and the surrogate's tractability guides efficient data acquisition for the BNN.

proposals.title: Kriging-Inspired Variance Minimization for Optimal Design in Bayesian Neural Network Active Learning
proposals.motivation: Active learning for Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) often relies on heuristic acquisition functions like entropy or BALD, which lack formal guarantees on the quality of the final predictive model. Geostatistical Kriging, in contrast, provides a principled framework for optimal experimental design based on minimizing the integrated prediction variance, a closed-form function of the spatial correlation model. Bridging this gap could yield more efficient and theoretically grounded data acquisition strategies for modern probabilistic models.
proposals.hypothesis: That formulating an active learning acquisition function for BNNs directly analogous to the Kriging variance—aiming to minimize the integrated posterior predictive variance over the input space—will lead to faster convergence and superior final model performance compared to standard heuristic methods.
proposals.method: We will leverage recent advances in tractable probabilistic inference, such as Einsum Networks or structured approximations within DynamicPPL, to enable efficient computation of the BNN's predictive covariance across the input domain. This covariance structure will define a kernel analogous to a variogram. We will then derive a sequential design criterion that selects the next point to minimize the total posterior variance, adapting the geostatistical 'minimization of the integrated Kriging variance' objective. The optimization will use automatic differentiation and probabilistic programming for scalable, gradient-based selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically motivated acquisition function for BNN active learning, grounded in optimal design theory. It will advance the field by moving beyond common heuristics, potentially offering new convergence insights. The integration of geostatistical principles with modern Bayesian deep learning represents a significant cross-disciplinary contribution.
proposals.novelty: While variance-based acquisition exists, the direct formal analogy to geostatistical Kriging theory and the explicit objective of minimizing integrated predictive variance for BNNs is novel. This approach systematically transfers a well-understood spatial sampling framework to the high-dimensional, non-parametric function space of neural networks.

proposals.title: Stratified Bayesian Batch Selection: A Probabilistic Circuit Approach to Active Learning via Ecological Survey Design
proposals.motivation: Active learning traditionally prioritizes uncertainty sampling, which can lead to biased, non-representative batches that fail to capture the underlying data manifold, harming model robustness and generalization. This problem mirrors challenges in ecological biodiversity surveys, where sampling only the most common species yields a poor estimate of true ecosystem diversity. Adapting systematic ecological survey designs to machine learning offers a principled way to select informative and diverse batches, improving data efficiency and reducing sampling bias.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly partitioning the feature space into distinct 'habitats' using probabilistic models and applying stratified sampling principles from ecology, we can construct active learning batches that are more representative of the data distribution, leading to faster convergence and better final model performance compared to standard uncertainty-based methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian framework where the unlabeled data manifold is modeled using a tractable probabilistic circuit, such as an Einsum Network, to perform fast, structured inference and partition the space into coherent clusters (strata). A dynamic probabilistic programming model (inspired by DynamicPPL) will manage the evolving belief state over these strata as labels are acquired. For each batch selection round, we will sample a balanced subset from each stratum, proportionally weighted by an acquisition function that blends predictive uncertainty (from the model) with stratum entropy (a diversity measure).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled batch active learning algorithm grounded in Bayesian nonparametrics and ecological design. It will provide a scalable, inference-driven alternative to heuristic diversity methods. We will release an open-source, automatic inference library integrating probabilistic circuits and dynamic models for reproducible batch selection research.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the formal adaptation of stratified ecological survey methodology into a Bayesian active learning framework, using tractable probabilistic circuits for efficient stratum definition and management. This moves beyond simple geometric diversity heuristics to a principled, model-based summarization of the data manifold for selection.

proposals.title: Disagreement-Constrained Co-Training with Probabilistic Circuits for Robust Semi-Supervised Active Learning
proposals.motivation: Semi-supervised learning (SSL) and active learning (AL) are crucial for leveraging abundant unlabeled data, but standard co-training can propagate errors through noisy pseudo-labels, while AL heuristics may query redundant points. A principled integration that ensures robustness and targets the most informative data points is lacking. This is particularly critical in high-stakes domains like treatment effect estimation, where model reliability is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: By restricting pseudo-label generation in a co-training framework to instances where two models with different inductive biases are confident but in disagreement, and then using an active learning strategy to query from within this constrained set, we can create a self-correcting loop that improves model performance and robustness more efficiently than either approach alone.
proposals.method: We will instantiate the framework using two tractable probabilistic models with distinct structures, such as an Einsum Network and a Bayesian neural network via DynamicPPL, providing clear uncertainty quantification. Their probabilistic outputs will define a 'disagreement-constrained' subset where predictions are confident but divergent. An active learning module, using a Bayesian acquisition function like entropy or BALD, will query labels specifically from this subset. The newly labeled data will then be used to retrain both models, closing the loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, robust algorithm for semi-supervised active learning that formally links model disagreement to query strategy. It will advance the field of learning with limited labels by demonstrating how to safely leverage unlabeled data while actively targeting collective model weaknesses. The use of probabilistic models ensures the approach is grounded in principled uncertainty reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit constraint that pseudo-labeling and active querying operate on the same, carefully defined data region—the intersection of high confidence and high disagreement—creating a synergistic, self-correcting mechanism not present in prior co-training or AL methods.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Capture-Recapture Framework for Joint Active and Semi-Supervised Learning
proposals.motivation: Semi-supervised learning (SSL) and active learning (AL) are both crucial for reducing labeling costs, but they are often applied sequentially or with loose coupling. Pre-2026, a key challenge is efficiently leveraging the vast unlabeled pool not just for acquisition but also for robust model training. A principled, unified framework that uses AL's uncertainty estimates to directly inform and weight the SSL loss is lacking, limiting data efficiency and performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the agreement of a probabilistic committee on unlabeled data as a 'recapture' event within a Bayesian framework can yield a principled measure of pseudo-label confidence, and integrating this measure into the SSL objective will improve learning efficiency and final model accuracy compared to decoupled or heuristic approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a Query-by-Committee (QbC) system where the committee comprises tractable probabilistic models, such as Einsum Networks, enabling fast, exact marginal inference. After an initial labeled batch ('capture'), committee predictions on the unlabeled pool form the 'recapture' sample. We will derive a Bayesian agreement score, analogous to a population estimate, quantifying the likelihood that an instance's predicted label is consistent. This score will then weight the instance's contribution in a DynamicPPL-implemented consistency regularization or pseudo-labeling loss, creating a single, joint training objective that unifies acquisition and semi-supervised training in an iterative loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically grounded framework for tightly integrated AL and SSL, improving data efficiency. It will contribute a new, interpretable uncertainty measure for pseudo-labeling derived from committee agreement. The implementation using tractable probabilistic circuits and dynamic probabilistic programming will demonstrate scalable, principled Bayesian inference for this combined learning setting.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the formal analogy to ecological capture-recapture, translating it into a Bayesian agreement measure that directly bridges AL acquisition and SSL loss weighting, moving beyond using committee disagreement solely for query selection. The integration of tractable probabilistic models for the committee enables exact inference of this measure, which is non-trivial with standard deep ensembles.

proposals.title: Provable Uncertainty for Deep Ensembles: A Kron-Factored Laplace Framework for Active Learning
proposals.motivation: Deep ensembles are a popular, scalable method for uncertainty quantification in neural networks but lack formal convergence guarantees, limiting their reliability in high-stakes active learning. Conversely, Bayesian methods like the Laplace approximation offer theoretical rigor but are computationally prohibitive for large modern architectures. Bridging this gap is crucial for developing efficient, trustworthy active learning agents.
proposals.hypothesis: That a deep ensemble, where each member's predictive distribution is analytically approximated via a Kron-factored Laplace posterior estimated with the KRONFIT algorithm, will yield aggregated uncertainty measures with provable convergence properties, leading to more statistically sound and efficient active learning.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid agent where each ensemble member is trained conventionally. For each member, we will compute a Kronecker-factored approximate Fisher information matrix at convergence and run the KRONFIT algorithm to obtain a Gaussian posterior approximation. We will derive formal convergence guarantees for KRONFIT in this context. The ensemble's predictive distribution will be a mixture of these analytic Gaussians, and active learning queries will be selected by maximizing the ensemble's aggregated entropy or BALD score.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first convergence proofs for uncertainty estimates derived from deep ensembles, grounding their empirical success in theory. It will deliver a scalable, practical active learning framework with stronger statistical guarantees than standard ensembles, validated on benchmarks from causal inference and structured prediction.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of the scalable Kronecker-factored Laplace approximation (a tool for single-model Bayesian inference) with deep ensembles to create a *provable* ensemble method is novel, as is the derivation of convergence proofs for the KRONFIT estimation process specifically within an ensemble-based active learning loop.

proposals.title: Ensemble-Based Uncertainty for Active Learning: A Tractable Probabilistic Framework for Dynamic Model Selection
proposals.motivation: Active Learning (AL) critically depends on reliable uncertainty estimates to select informative data points, but Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) are computationally prohibitive for many real-world tasks. Deep ensembles offer a simpler, high-performing alternative for uncertainty quantification, but their application to AL lacks a principled, scalable probabilistic framework for dynamically managing the ensemble's structure and interpreting its predictive distribution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing a deep ensemble as a tractable, dynamic probabilistic model, we can develop an AL strategy that matches or exceeds the sample efficiency of Bayesian methods while offering superior computational scalability and interpretable uncertainty decomposition.
proposals.method: We will treat each ensemble member as a component in a mixture model, leveraging tractable probabilistic circuits (e.g., Einsum Networks) to efficiently represent and infer over the joint ensemble prediction. Using DynamicPPL, we will define a prior over ensemble expansion (branching) and member specialization, enabling automatic, inference-driven growth of the ensemble structure tailored to the AL acquisition function. The acquisition strategy will utilize the ensemble's predictive entropy, decomposed into aleatoric and epistemic components via the probabilistic circuit representation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable AL algorithm that provides robust uncertainty estimation without the cost of BNNs. It will contribute a formal probabilistic interpretation of deep ensembles for sequential decision-making and release an open-source library integrating DynamicPPL with automatic ensemble structure learning for AL.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in unifying deep ensembles, dynamic probabilistic programming, and tractable probabilistic circuits to create an adaptive, interpretable ensemble model whose structure evolves automatically during the AL loop, moving beyond static ensemble methods.

proposals.title: Active Bayesian Learning for High-Resolution 3D Medical Imaging with Tractable Probabilistic Circuits
proposals.motivation: High-resolution 3D medical scans (e.g., MRI, CT) present a massive data annotation bottleneck for supervised learning. Current active learning methods struggle with the extreme dimensionality and computational cost of these volumes, limiting their use in clinical settings where expert labeling is scarce and expensive. A scalable, data-efficient framework is urgently needed to make AI-assisted diagnosis feasible for detailed volumetric analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a tractable, factorized probabilistic model for structured uncertainty quantification with a novel, branching acquisition function, we can achieve sample-efficient active learning on gigavoxel-scale 3D images while maintaining computational tractability.
proposals.method: We will adapt the fast inference and learning framework of Einsum Networks (EiNets) to model high-dimensional, structured uncertainty over 3D patches. A hierarchical, branching probabilistic model will be developed within DynamicPPL to manage multi-scale dependencies. For the acquisition strategy, we will extend the treatment policy learning concept from bipartite embeddings to a sequential decision process, where the policy selects the most informative sub-volume (branch) to query next, balancing exploration and exploitation in the high-resolution space.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable active learning system specifically designed for ultra-high-resolution 3D medical data. It will advance the field of Bayesian deep learning by demonstrating how tractable probabilistic circuits can be practically deployed for massive structured data. The resulting framework will provide a principled, sample-efficient path for annotating complex volumetric scans.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of tractable probabilistic circuits (EiNets) for scalable 3D uncertainty estimation with a dynamic, branching acquisition policy, moving beyond standard low-resolution or 2D active learning approaches. This addresses the core computational and statistical challenges of extreme-scale volumetric data in a unified Bayesian framework.

proposals.title: Co-Select: Joint Bayesian Optimization of Pruning Masks and Informative Data Subsets for Robust Model Compression
proposals.motivation: Model compression via pruning is essential for deploying neural networks on resource-constrained devices, but standard methods often degrade robustness and rely on the full, potentially redundant, training set. Pre-2026, data subset selection and pruning are typically decoupled, leading to suboptimal compression where the pruned architecture is not matched to a minimally sufficient, informative dataset. This work addresses the need for efficient, robust compression by jointly optimizing the model structure and the data it learns from.
proposals.hypothesis: A joint, probabilistic search over the space of pruning masks and training data subsets can identify synergistic pairs that yield compressed models with superior robustness and generalization compared to sequential or independent optimization, as the retained data is maximally informative for the specific sparse final architecture.
proposals.method: We frame the search as a structured probabilistic inference problem. We will develop a Bayesian optimization framework where the acquisition function evaluates candidate (mask, subset) pairs. Each pair is evaluated by training a sparse network (using a probabilistic circuit or tractable density estimator for efficiency) on the subset and assessing its robustness on a held-out validation set. The search will leverage recent advances in automatic differentiation for probabilistic programming (e.g., DynamicPPL) to handle the dynamic model structure and branching decisions inherent in the mask-space exploration, efficiently navigating the combinatorial space.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, principled algorithm for robust model compression that unifies structure and data learning. It will advance the field by providing a method to produce smaller models that are specifically stable and accurate on a core, non-redundant dataset. The resulting framework will be a step towards automated, resource-efficient machine learning systems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the joint, validation-driven co-optimization of the pruning mask and the training data subset, treating them as interdependent variables in a single search problem, rather than treating data selection as a pre-processing step independent of the final model's architecture.

proposals.title: Trophic Data Pruning: A Probabilistic Graphical Framework for Identifying Redundant Training Samples
proposals.motivation: Large-scale datasets are crucial for modern machine learning but contain redundancy, leading to inefficiency in training and model bloat. Pre-2026 data pruning methods often rely on simple heuristics like loss or gradient magnitude, lacking a principled, relational understanding of how data points collectively influence a model. A systematic, theoretically grounded framework for identifying the least informative data is needed for efficient model compression and data curation.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling training data as a trophic network where influence flows between samples based on shared effects on model parameters, we can identify 'trophically weak' data points whose removal minimally degrades model performance, analogous to removing non-keystone species in an ecosystem.
proposals.method: We will construct a probabilistic data-influence graph: nodes are data points, and edge weights are defined by pairwise metrics such as gradient dot products or predictive influence via the Fisher Information Matrix. Using tools from probabilistic graphical models and network analysis (e.g., centrality measures, community detection, and simulated 'energy' flow), we will develop an algorithm to rank data points by their trophic importance. This will be integrated into a Bayesian active learning loop, using automatic inference (e.g., via DynamicPPL) to quantify the uncertainty of pruning decisions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, ecology-inspired framework for data pruning with strong theoretical underpinnings in network science and Bayesian inference. It will yield a practical algorithm for dataset compression that directly targets functional redundancy, potentially accelerating training and creating leaner, high-performance models.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in framing data pruning as an ecological network analysis problem, moving beyond point-wise scores to a relational model of data influence. It uniquely inverts active learning paradigms to seek the least informative data, using probabilistic inference for robust pruning decisions.

proposals.title: Active Bayesian Compression of Multi-Relational Graphs via Tensor Decomposition and Probabilistic Pruning
proposals.motivation: Large multi-relational graphs, such as knowledge graphs and social networks, are ubiquitous but pose significant challenges for storage, inference, and learning due to their scale and complexity. Pre-2026 methods for graph compression, like tensor decomposition, often produce static models that do not actively optimize the training process or the final model's sparsity. A principled, data-efficient method to jointly compress the model architecture and the required training data is needed for scalable probabilistic reasoning on these structured domains.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing a multi-relational graph as a probabilistic tensor model, we can use Bayesian measures of factor importance to actively and iteratively prune both insignificant model components (tensor factors) and their corresponding, least informative training edges, achieving significant compression without substantial loss in relational structure fidelity.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic tensor factorization model for the adjacency tensor of a multi-relational graph, building on tractable probabilistic circuit frameworks like Einsum Networks for efficient inference. We will derive a Bayesian measure of importance for each factor in the decomposition, akin to automatic relevance determination. An active learning loop will use this measure to rank and prune low-importance factors. Crucially, we will extend this to data pruning by identifying the node-pair instances (edges) whose likelihood is least affected by the pruned factors, removing them from the training set in a 'branching' process that yields a compressed dataset for subsequent model refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end framework for active compression of relational data, reducing both model size and training data requirements. It will advance scalable Bayesian learning for graphs by introducing a principled, inference-driven method for joint model and data sparsification. The resulting algorithms will enable efficient storage and faster inference on massive multi-relational networks.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of active learning for data pruning with tensor-based model compression within a unified Bayesian framework is novel. Unlike static decomposition methods, our approach iteratively and jointly prunes the model and the dataset based on probabilistic measures of informativeness, creating a highly compact and efficient representation.

proposals.title: Dual-Use Active Learning: A Bayesian Framework for Identifying Redundant Data for Model Compression
proposals.motivation: Model compression is essential for deploying large neural networks on resource-constrained devices, but current pruning and distillation methods often rely on heuristics or uniform sampling of the training data for calibration. Conversely, active learning focuses on identifying the most informative data points. This project inverts this paradigm to address a critical inefficiency: a significant portion of training data may be redundant for the compression task, wasting computational resources and potentially harming final model performance through noisy calibration.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing data selection for post-training compression as an active learning problem, we can use Bayesian measures of model uncertainty and influence to systematically identify and exclude the least informative training samples for the compression objective, leading to more accurate and robust compressed models.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic framework where the target is the performance of the compressed model, not the original model. Building on Ghahramani's work on tractable probabilistic circuits (Einsum Networks) and scalable inference (DynamicPPL), we will treat the pruning mask or student model parameters as latent variables. We will compute the expected information gain (EIG) of each training datum on these compression parameters, using efficient approximations from automatic and branching probabilistic inference techniques. Data points with the lowest EIG will be pruned from the calibration set used for compression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, Bayesian methodology for data-efficient model compression, reducing the computational cost of post-training compression routines. It will also contribute a novel 'dual-use' perspective to active learning, expanding its application beyond pure data acquisition to include data curation for downstream tasks.
proposals.novelty: While active learning selects data for training, we invert it to de-select data for compression. The key novelty is a joint probabilistic model that links data influence directly to the compressed model's performance, moving beyond heuristics like random or core-set selection.

proposals.title: Seamline Selection for Batch Acquisition: A Probabilistic Circuit Approach to Massively Parallel Active Learning
proposals.motivation: Massively parallel data labeling, enabled by platforms like Amazon Mechanical Turk or distributed scientific teams, is bottlenecked by inefficient batch active learning (AL) strategies. Pre-2026 batch AL methods, like BatchBALD or cluster-based diversity, struggle to scale to thousands of points, often selecting redundant samples that waste parallel labeling resources. This inefficiency is critical in domains like remote sensing or genomics, where labeling is expensive but parallelization is possible.
proposals.hypothesis: By formalizing the batch acquisition problem through the lens of satellite image mosaicking and seamline detection, we can develop a probabilistic acquisition function that selects large, non-redundant batches with provable guarantees on global information gain and diversity.
proposals.method: We will treat the model's predictive uncertainty surface as a 'landscape' to be imaged. Inspired by seamline detection algorithms that select optimal tile overlaps, we will develop a tractable probabilistic model over the data pool using an Einsum Network (EiNet) to efficiently compute joint information measures. The acquisition function will be formulated as a dynamic probabilistic program in DynamicPPL, optimizing for a set of 'seams' that partition the uncertainty landscape into maximally informative, minimally overlapping batches. This involves a novel branching inference procedure to explore combinatorial selections.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, theoretically grounded batch AL algorithm capable of handling orders-of-magnitude larger batch sizes. It will advance the automation of large-scale data curation for Bayesian models. We will provide an open-source implementation integrated with the Turing.jl ecosystem, demonstrating significant speed and performance gains on remote sensing and medical image datasets.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the cross-disciplinary analogy to remote sensing mosaicking, leading to a seamline optimization objective not present in prior AL work. The technical integration of tractable probabilistic circuits (EiNets) for joint information estimation with dynamic probabilistic programming for batch construction is a unique synthesis of Ghahramani group methods.

proposals.title: Scalable Bayesian Batch Active Learning via Tractable Probabilistic Circuits and Parallel Inference
proposals.motivation: Modern machine learning applications, such as large-scale causal inference for treatment policy learning, often operate in data-rich environments where labeling is expensive but can be massively parallelized. Traditional batch active learning methods struggle to scale to selecting thousands of points simultaneously, as they rely on greedy, sequential approximations or become computationally intractable. This bottleneck limits the efficiency of learning from large, unlabeled datasets in fields like healthcare and recommender systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging the fast, exact inference and tractable density estimation of probabilistic circuits (e.g., Einsum Networks) within a Bayesian framework, we can develop a batch acquisition function that efficiently optimizes for global information gain across very large batch sizes, overcoming the scalability limits of existing methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel acquisition function based on the mutual information between the model parameters and the batch of candidate points. The key innovation is using an Einsum Network as a surrogate for the posterior predictive distribution, enabling rapid, parallel computation of entropy and conditional entropy for massive candidate sets. We will integrate this with DynamicPPL for flexible model specification and use stochastic optimization with automatic differentiation to select batches. A branching exploration strategy will diversify the batch across regions of high uncertainty and high expected model change.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable algorithm for massive batch active learning, directly applicable to large-scale causal and probabilistic modeling tasks. It will extend the utility of tractable probabilistic models to a new domain of decision-making under uncertainty and release an open-source, high-performance implementation integrated with the Pyro/NumPyro ecosystem.
proposals.novelty: The combination of tractable probabilistic circuits for scalable entropy calculations with dynamic probabilistic programming for model flexibility is novel for active learning. This moves beyond Gaussian approximations or small-batch greedy methods to a truly parallel, global batch optimization scheme.

proposals.title: Bayesian Interpretations of Dropout via Tractable Non-Gaussian Priors
proposals.motivation: The seminal work of Gal & Ghahramani (2016) established dropout as approximate variational inference in deep Gaussian processes (GPs) with a specific prior. This Gaussian prior assumption is restrictive and may not capture the true inductive biases or uncertainty patterns desirable for many tasks. A more general Bayesian interpretation, linking dropout to a broader class of tractable prior distributions, would enhance the theoretical understanding and practical utility of dropout as a Bayesian deep learning tool.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dropout training in neural networks can be derived as approximate inference under a family of tractable, non-Gaussian prior distributions, leading to more flexible and expressive uncertainty estimates without sacrificing scalability.
proposals.method: We will leverage the framework of tractable probabilistic circuits, specifically Einsum Networks (EiNets), to construct expressive yet computationally manageable prior distributions over network weights. Using DynamicPPL for flexible model specification, we will formulate a variational inference objective where the dropout mask distribution acts as the variational posterior. The core technical challenge will be to derive the conditions under which the dropout training objective emerges as the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) for these new priors, potentially using automatic differentiation variational inference (ADVI) techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the theoretical foundation of Bayesian deep learning by decoupling dropout from the Gaussian process prior. It will provide practitioners with a principled framework to select or design dropout schemes matched to specific prior beliefs about model parameters, potentially improving predictive uncertainty and model robustness in applications like treatment effect estimation where reliable uncertainty is critical.
proposals.novelty: While the Gaussian process interpretation of dropout is well-known, deriving its connection to a broad, learnable class of tractable non-Gaussian priors via probabilistic circuits is a novel and unexplored direction that bridges scalable deep learning and flexible Bayesian modeling.

proposals.title: Scalable Bayesian Inference for Deep Architectures: A Benchmarking Framework on Large-Scale Vision and Language Tasks
proposals.motivation: While Bayesian deep learning offers principled uncertainty quantification, its application has been largely confined to small datasets (e.g., MNIST, CIFAR) and shallow architectures due to computational and statistical intractability. To realize its promise in real-world, high-stakes applications, rigorous validation on modern, large-scale problems is critically needed. This gap limits our understanding of the performance and scalability of contemporary Bayesian approximations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that recent advances in tractable probabilistic models and inference engines, such as structured probabilistic circuits and dynamic probabilistic programming, can be scaled to provide effective uncertainty estimates and robust performance on large-scale datasets and deep neural architectures without prohibitive computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified benchmarking framework using DynamicPPL for flexible model specification and automated inference. The core approach will involve integrating structured, tractable priors and approximate posteriors inspired by Einsum Networks into deep ResNet and Transformer architectures. We will systematically evaluate on large-scale vision datasets (e.g., ImageNet) and language tasks (e.g., WikiText), measuring predictive performance, calibration, and uncertainty quality against frequentist baselines and simpler Bayesian approximations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive empirical analysis of scalable Bayesian methods on industry-standard benchmarks, establishing clear performance trade-offs. It will deliver open-source, modular code for the community to benchmark new Bayesian approximations. The findings will guide the future development of practical Bayesian deep learning systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, large-scale application and evaluation of modern tractable probabilistic learning frameworks (probabilistic circuits, dynamic PPL) to deep, high-dimensional architectures beyond proof-of-concept datasets, an undertaking not yet comprehensively addressed in the literature.

proposals.title: Bayesian Calibration of Dropout: A Probabilistic Circuit Framework for Reliable Uncertainty Quantification
proposals.motivation: Dropout is widely used as a practical approximation to Bayesian inference in deep learning, but its uncertainty estimates are often poorly calibrated, failing to match true posterior confidence. This mis-calibration limits its reliability in high-stakes applications like medical treatment effect estimation and autonomous systems. A principled, scalable method to improve dropout's calibration is needed to make its uncertainty trustworthy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the calibration error of dropout-based uncertainty arises from a mismatch between the implicit variational distribution and the true posterior, and that this can be corrected by learning a structured, tractable probabilistic model of the dropout-induced predictive distribution.
proposals.method: We will frame the ensemble of subnetworks sampled via dropout as a latent variable model and learn a tractable probabilistic circuit (PC), specifically an Einsum Network, to model its predictive distribution. This PC will be trained to maximize the marginal likelihood of predictions, effectively performing Bayesian model averaging over the dropout ensemble. We will integrate this with automatic differentiation and inference in DynamicPPL to enable efficient learning of the calibration model's parameters, ensuring the refined uncertainty estimates are both accurate and computationally scalable.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded, practical tool for obtaining well-calibrated uncertainty from standard dropout networks without changing the training procedure. It will advance the field of Bayesian deep learning by bridging the gap between approximate inference techniques and reliable probabilistic reasoning. The resulting method will be directly applicable to improving uncertainty in causal effect estimation and other structured prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted dropout's calibration issues, our approach is novel in using the expressive power of tractable probabilistic circuits to explicitly model and correct the ensemble distribution, moving beyond simple post-hoc scaling. This creates a formal, learnable connection between dropout and structured Bayesian inference.

proposals.title: Active Bayesian Learning with Concrete Dropout for Efficient Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from observational data is crucial for personalized decision-making in fields like medicine and economics, but often requires expensive, high-quality labeled outcomes. Active learning can reduce labeling costs, but standard methods lack principled uncertainty quantification for complex, high-dimensional data. Pre-2026, dropout has been established as a practical approximation to Bayesian inference in deep networks, providing a scalable source of uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: That an active learning strategy, using uncertainty estimates derived from Concrete Dropout networks within a Bayesian treatment effect model, can select data points for labeling that significantly improve estimation performance and data efficiency compared to passive or heuristic acquisition functions.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic probabilistic model for treatment effect estimation using the DynamicPPL framework, incorporating a deep neural network with Concrete Dropout for automatic tuning of dropout probabilities. The model's posterior predictive uncertainty, obtained via Monte Carlo dropout at inference time, will drive a Bayesian active learning loop. We will implement and compare acquisition functions such as BALD (Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement) and predictive entropy, iteratively querying the most informative (treatment, covariate) pairs for outcome labeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, fully probabilistic active learning framework for causal inference, directly linking approximate Bayesian deep learning with optimal experimental design. It will yield a practical method for reducing the cost of data collection in studies of heterogeneous treatment effects and demonstrate the utility of dropout networks beyond passive prediction.
proposals.novelty: While dropout for uncertainty is known, its integration into an active learning loop specifically for optimizing the data efficiency of treatment effect estimation models is novel. The combination of DynamicPPL's flexibility for structured models with the automated uncertainty of Concrete Dropout creates a unique, end-to-end Bayesian active learning system.

proposals.title: Sequential Monte Carlo for Adaptive Dropout: A Particle Filtering Approach to Bayesian Neural Network Training
proposals.motivation: Dropout is a widely used regularization technique, and its interpretation as approximate Bayesian inference in deep Gaussian processes provides a principled link to uncertainty quantification. However, standard dropout training yields a single, static dropout mask distribution, which may not capture the evolving, multi-modal posterior over network weights during stochastic optimization. This limits the quality of the inferred posterior approximation for robust prediction and calibration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the training trajectory of a neural network with dropout as a sequential state-space model and applying Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) methods will yield a richer, adaptive posterior approximation over weights compared to static dropout, leading to improved predictive performance and uncertainty estimates.
proposals.method: We will frame the iterative stochastic gradient descent updates with dropout as a state evolution, where the state is the network's weight vector and the random dropout masks are auxiliary variables. A particle filter will be used to maintain and resample a set of weight particles, with importance weights updated based on mini-batch likelihoods. We will leverage recent advances in scalable, automatic probabilistic programming (e.g., DynamicPPL) to implement the SMC sampler efficiently. The proposal distribution for new particles will be informed by the gradient update, creating an adaptive branching process over plausible weight trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, tractable online inference algorithm for Bayesian neural networks that unifies dropout training with formal sequential Monte Carlo. It will provide a more accurate posterior approximation than standard dropout, enhancing predictive performance, uncertainty quantification, and model calibration on benchmark tasks.
proposals.novelty: While SMC is established for dynamic models and particle-based optimization exists, its direct application to track the evolving posterior during dropout training—treating each training step as a time step in a filtering problem—is a novel synthesis of scalable deep learning and principled Bayesian inference.

proposals.title: Bayesian Optimization with Dropout Networks as Tractable Probabilistic Surrogates
proposals.motivation: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a powerful framework for optimizing expensive black-box functions, but its performance critically depends on the choice of surrogate model. Gaussian processes (GPs), the standard surrogate, struggle with high-dimensional, structured data and non-stationary functions. Recent advances in scalable Bayesian deep learning, particularly dropout-based uncertainty estimation, offer a promising alternative for building flexible, high-capacity surrogates that can capture complex structure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dropout neural network, trained as a tractable probabilistic surrogate, can outperform standard GP-based BO on high-dimensional and structured optimization problems by providing more expressive predictive distributions and better uncertainty quantification.
proposals.method: We will develop a BO framework using Monte Carlo (MC) dropout networks as the surrogate model. The method leverages the network's predictive mean and variance, derived from multiple stochastic forward passes, to compute an acquisition function (e.g., Expected Improvement). To ensure scalability and tractable inference, we will integrate architectural insights from tractable probabilistic circuits (e.g., Einsum Networks) to structure the network layers, and utilize dynamic probabilistic programming (via DynamicPPL) for flexible model specification and efficient gradient-based training of the surrogate.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable BO algorithm capable of handling complex, high-dimensional search spaces. It will advance the integration of deep learning and Bayesian optimization, providing a practical tool for scientific and engineering design. Furthermore, it will offer new theoretical and empirical insights into using approximate Bayesian neural networks for sequential decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While dropout has been used for uncertainty estimation, its systematic integration as a structured, tractable probabilistic surrogate within the BO loop—informed by recent advances in probabilistic circuits and dynamic probabilistic modeling—is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond treating the network as a black-box uncertainty estimator and explicitly designs its architecture for efficient inference within the optimization task.

proposals.title: Information-Theoretic Uncertainty Quantification for Bayesian Deep Learning via Dropout Ensembles
proposals.motivation: Dropout is widely used as an approximate Bayesian inference technique in deep learning, but its uncertainty estimates are often heuristic. A principled, information-theoretic quantification of epistemic uncertainty from dropout distributions is lacking, limiting its reliability in high-stakes applications like treatment effect estimation and model selection. Grounding this in information theory would provide a more rigorous foundation for Bayesian deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: That the mutual information between the model's predictive distribution and its dropout-induced posterior distribution provides a more robust and theoretically grounded measure of epistemic uncertainty than standard predictive entropy or variance estimates from dropout ensembles.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework to compute the mutual information I(y; ω | x, D) = H[E_{p(ω|D)}[p(y|x, ω)]] - E_{p(ω|D)}[H[p(y|x, ω)]], where ω represents the dropout masks. This will be implemented efficiently by leveraging branching and automatic differentiation within a dynamic probabilistic programming framework (e.g., extending DynamicPPL) to sample from the dropout distribution. We will integrate this measure into tractable probabilistic circuit architectures for structured data and evaluate it on causal inference tasks involving continuous treatment policies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically sound, information-theoretic metric for epistemic uncertainty in dropout-based Bayesian neural networks. It will enhance model interpretability and performance in uncertainty-sensitive domains like causal effect estimation and automated model selection, bridging gaps between deep learning and probabilistic machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While mutual information has been proposed for other Bayesian neural network approximations, its direct application and efficient computation specifically for the dropout ensemble distribution—treating it as a proper Bayesian posterior—is novel. The integration with dynamic probabilistic programming for scalable inference on this measure is also a new technical contribution.

proposals.title: Bayesian Architecture Search for Minimizing Predictive Variance Under a Parameter Budget
proposals.motivation: In resource-constrained settings, practitioners must balance model capacity against predictive reliability. Current neural architecture search (NAS) primarily optimizes for predictive accuracy, often neglecting the quantification of uncertainty, which is critical for safe deployment in fields like healthcare and autonomous systems. A principled method to design architectures that explicitly minimize predictive uncertainty for a fixed parameter budget is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing architecture search as a Bayesian optimization problem over a structured, tractable space of probabilistic circuits, we can discover neural network architectures that achieve significantly lower predictive variance than standard architectures of equivalent parameter count.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel NAS framework where the search space is defined by branching, structured probabilistic circuits, inspired by Einsum Networks for tractable inference. The objective will be to minimize a variational bound on expected predictive variance, regularized by a hard constraint on the total number of model parameters. Optimization will be performed via automatic differentiation through the architecture parameters within a DynamicPPL-like framework, enabling efficient gradient-based inference in this structured, dynamic model space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical algorithm for designing trustworthy, capacity-efficient models, directly linking Bayesian uncertainty quantification to automated architecture design. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating architectures on their uncertainty-efficiency trade-off and extend the principles of tractable probabilistic models to the domain of neural architecture search.
proposals.novelty: While NAS and Bayesian deep learning are established, jointly optimizing architecture for parameter efficiency and predictive uncertainty using the formalism of tractable probabilistic circuits is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond using Bayesian methods solely for inference in fixed architectures to actively shaping the architecture for uncertainty minimization.

proposals.title: Online Bayesian Learning with Adaptive Dropout for Non-Stationary Data Streams
proposals.motivation: Modern applications, from sensor networks to adaptive treatment policies, generate continuous, non-stationary data streams. Pre-2026 Bayesian deep learning methods for uncertainty quantification, like Monte Carlo dropout, are predominantly designed for batch or stationary online settings, lacking principled mechanisms to adapt their uncertainty estimates to underlying distribution shifts. This limits their reliability in dynamic real-world systems where model confidence must reflect current, not historical, data regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing dropout probabilities as time-varying, adaptively learned parameters within a streaming variational inference framework, we can develop a scalable Bayesian approximation that maintains accurate predictive uncertainty for non-stationary data.
proposals.method: We will extend the DynamicPPL framework for efficient online variational inference in dynamic probabilistic models. The core innovation is to treat the dropout mask application in a neural network as a stochastic process, parameterizing per-unit dropout rates with a branching latent variable model that evolves via Bayesian filtering. These rates will be optimized jointly with model weights using an online version of the local reparameterization trick, minimizing a streaming evidence lower bound (ELBO) that incorporates a KL penalty encouraging adaptation to recent data. Inference will leverage automatic differentiation within a probabilistic programming paradigm for flexibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a theoretically grounded, scalable algorithm for online uncertainty quantification under distribution shift. It will provide the community with an open-source implementation within the DynamicPPL/Stan ecosystem, enabling robust Bayesian learning for streaming applications in causal inference and beyond.
proposals.novelty: While dropout is a established Bayesian approximation, its online adaptation for non-stationarity is unexplored. The novelty lies in unifying streaming variational inference with adaptive stochastic regularization, moving beyond fixed dropout rates to learn a dynamic uncertainty representation.

ideas.title: Topology-Aware Active Learning on Graphs with Heterogeneous Structure
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel active learning paradigm for graph-structured data, where acquisition functions are designed based on the local topological properties of the data points (nodes), such as their degree and community structure, rather than model uncertainty. By integrating the concept of non-stationary degree distributions, the method recognizes that nodes in densely connected hubs may require a different sampling strategy than nodes in sparse, peripheral regions of the graph. This topology-aware approach allows the learner to strategically query nodes that are structurally diverse or critical for information flow, effectively using the graph's inherent heterogeneity as a proxy for data representativeness and influence. The result is a highly scalable and model-agnostic active learning strategy particularly suited for large-scale networks like social graphs, citation networks, or biological interactomes, where Bayesian uncertainty quantification is often intractable.

ideas.title: Theoretically Grounded Deep Ensembles for Active Learning via Kron-Factored Likelihood Approximation
ideas.description: This research fuses the practical efficiency of deep ensembles for uncertainty quantification with the theoretical rigor of convergence proofs for the KRONFIT algorithm. The core idea is to use a Kron-factored Laplace approximation, estimated via KRONFIT, to analytically approximate the predictive distribution of each member in a deep ensemble, rather than relying solely on empirical variance. By providing formal convergence guarantees for the KRONFIT estimation process within this ensemble context, the work establishes a solid theoretical foundation for the ensemble's aggregated uncertainty measures. This results in a hybrid, theoretically grounded active learning agent that is both computationally scalable for modern neural networks and equipped with provable statistical guarantees on its query strategy's performance.

ideas.title: Active Tensor Pruning for Multi-Relational Graph Compression
ideas.description: This research fuses active learning for model compression with tensor decomposition for multi-relational graphs. The core idea is to use a tensor factorization of a multi-relational network to not only fit a compact Kronecker-like model but also to analyze the factor importance. This analysis actively identifies which relational edge types and which specific node-pair instances (training data points for the graph model) are least informative for the overall relational structure. By iteratively pruning these insignificant tensor components and their corresponding training edges, the method compresses both the model architecture and the required training dataset, leading to highly efficient storage and inference for large, complex networks.

ideas.title: Active Few-Shot Initiation with a Constrained Unlabeled Data Pool
ideas.description: This research direction develops active learning strategies specifically for the critical initiation phase of few-shot learning, under the strict constraint that the pool of available unlabeled data is extremely small and non-replenishable. It moves beyond standard active learning assumptions by treating the unlabeled pool as a scarce, exhaustible resource that must be fully consumed to construct the initial few-shot support set. The core challenge is to design acquisition functions that jointly optimize for immediate informativeness, long-term representativeness for the task, and robustness given the impending complete depletion of the pool. This results in a holistic 'cold-start' protocol for few-shot learning where the very first examples selected are deterministically the final training set, merging dataset construction and model initialization into a single, optimal selection step.

ideas.title: Active Learning via Representational Disagreement and Density Peaks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology for discovering new species, which relies on identifying specimens that are both distinct and locally abundant, to the problem of active learning without explicit model uncertainty. The analogous ecological method avoids complex probabilistic models of biodiversity by focusing on morphological dissimilarity and population density. Adapted to deep active learning, the proposed acquisition function operates in the model's learned representation (latent) space. It selects unlabeled data points that are both far from the centroids of existing labeled clusters (ensuring novelty or potential for model disagreement) and situated in dense regions of the unlabeled data manifold (ensuring they are representative and not mere artifacts or outliers). This dual criterion provides a non-Bayesian, geometry-based heuristic for querying informative samples that challenge and expand the model's current knowledge boundaries.

ideas.title: Scale down: Miniature graph generation for testing
ideas.description: Generate tiny, representative graph motifs that preserve Kronecker properties for algorithm unit testing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Kronecker Graphs: An Approach to Modeling Networks

ideas.title: Scale to extremely high-resolution 3D medical images
ideas.description: Adapt the framework for active learning on massive 3D image volumes like MRI or CT scans.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deep Bayesian Active Learning with Image Data

ideas.title: Adapt multigrid methods for faster simulation
ideas.description: Use numerical multigrid techniques to accelerate the simulation of Kronecker graphs at multiple scales.
ideas.sourceTitle: Kronecker Graphs: An Approach to Modeling Networks

ideas.title: Employ compressive sensing for initiator recovery
ideas.description: Leverage sparse signal recovery techniques to estimate the initiator matrix from highly incomplete network observations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Kronecker Graphs: An Approach to Modeling Networks

ideas.title: Prove KRONFIT convergence via analogy to Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm stability theory
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to develop theoretical convergence guarantees for the KRONFIT estimation algorithm by drawing an analogy to the well-established convergence proofs for the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm in statistical machine learning. The analogous solution involves the Minorize-Maximize (MM) framework, where convergence is proven by demonstrating that each iterative step maximizes a surrogate function that minorizes the true objective, ensuring monotonic improvement, boundedness of parameter sequences, and eventual convergence to a fixed point. Adapted to KRONFIT, the work will involve: 1) formally showing that KRONFIT's iterative updates constitute an MM algorithm for the Kronecker product model's likelihood, 2) proving the boundedness of the sequence of Kronecker factor estimates under reasonable conditions, and 3) applying convergence theorems for monotone algorithms to guarantee that KRONFIT converges to a local optimum or a stationary point, thereby providing the missing formal statistical guarantees for this computationally efficient method.

ideas.title: Multi-Property Coverage: Generating Minimal Graph Test Suites for Algorithm Validation
ideas.description: This research designs a method to generate a minimal, comprehensive suite of tiny graphs for unit testing graph algorithms. The core innovation is an automated search that constructs or selects graphs to maximize coverage of a diverse property space—including spectral, structural, and combinatorial features—and known pathological cases. The resulting test suite is validated by ensuring that an algorithm's performance (correctness, runtime anomaly) on each minimal graph is a reliable indicator of its performance on larger, more complex graphs from real-world and synthetic families. This moves beyond single-model fidelity to provide robust, efficient, and interpretable testing.

ideas.title: Co-Select: Joint Optimization of Pruning Masks and Informative Subsets for Robust Compression
ideas.description: This method reframes model compression as a joint search problem for both an optimal pruning mask and a minimal, maximally informative training subset. Instead of pre-selecting data based on the full model, it employs an iterative, validation-driven process that trains candidate sparse networks on different data subsets and evaluates their post-pruning robustness. The goal is to identify the synergistic pair of pruning strategy and core dataset that yields a compressed model with minimal performance degradation. This directly ensures the retained data is informative specifically for the final compressed architecture's stability and accuracy.

ideas.title: Apply Geostatistical Kriging Variance for Bayesian Neural Network Active Learning
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from geostatistics, where the Kriging variance—a direct function of the spatial correlation model and sampling locations—provides a principled, closed-form measure for optimal design of experiments to map a spatial field. The analogous solution uses this variance to guide where to sample next. We adapt this to Bayesian neural network active learning by formulating an acquisition function based on the predictive variance of the BNN's posterior, computed across the input space. This approach aims to transfer the theoretical grounding of geostatistical sampling theory (e.g., minimizing integrated prediction variance) to the BNN context, moving beyond common heuristics like entropy or BALD, and could lead to new convergence guarantees for active learning with complex, non-parametric models like neural networks.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Surrogate Processes for Provable Active Learning with Bayesian Neural Networks
ideas.description: This approach circumvents the intractability of BNNs for active learning by meta-learning a tractable stochastic process that acts as a faithful surrogate for the BNN's predictive distribution. The system jointly trains the BNN and a meta-model that, given a dataset context, produces the parameters of a well-calibrated surrogate process (e.g., a Gaussian process with a structured kernel). Active learning acquisition functions and theoretical guarantees are applied directly to this surrogate, which is efficiently updated. The selected data points then refine both the underlying BNN and the meta-model, creating a robust, theoretically grounded active learning loop for complex neural network models.

ideas.title: Dual: Active learning for model compression
ideas.description: Inversely, use the framework to identify which training data points are least informative for model pruning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deep Bayesian Active Learning with Image Data
